acts.title: The Hollow Crown
acts.description: The novel opens in the first week of April 1841, with Harrison's death shattering the assumptions of every power-holder in Washington. Tyler receives the news in Williamsburg and rides north into a constitutional vacuum—no precedent exists for what he is about to claim. Clay, in Kentucky, receives the news with barely concealed satisfaction, certain that the compliant Vice President will prove even more tractable than the old general. Webster, already in Washington, watches the cabinet fracture into confusion and begins his own calculation. Tyler's arrival, his insistence on taking a full presidential oath, and his proclamation of complete executive authority constitute the inciting incident: a man whom no one elected to the presidency seizes it absolutely, and every alliance in the capital must be redrawn. The act ends with Tyler's first cabinet meeting, where he retains Harrison's men but makes clear—through silence, posture, and a single devastating sentence—that he will not be governed by them. Clay's response, conveyed through intermediaries, is the turning point: he will come to Washington and bring the man to heel.
dramatic_beats: Harrison's death and the constitutional void it opens—rendered through Webster's POV as the cabinet gathers in confusion over whether Tyler is President or Acting President
Tyler receives the death notice in Williamsburg; his private terror and public composure as he resolves to claim the full office
Tyler's dawn arrival in Washington and the swearing-in before Judge Cranch—the oath that no Vice President has ever taken, witnessed by men who are not sure it is legitimate
Tyler's proclamation of full presidential authority, drafted in his own hand, asserting constitutional ground that has never been tested
The first cabinet meeting: Tyler listens, asks no questions, and closes with a statement that executive power is not subject to cabinet vote
Clay dispatches instructions to his Washington allies—the first move in a campaign to reduce Tyler to a figurehead, ending the act on a note of gathering menace
acts.stakes_level: personal and constitutional—the question is whether one man's claim to the presidency will hold against the combined skepticism of the political establishment

acts.title: The Architecture of Obedience
acts.description: May through mid-July 1841. Clay arrives in Washington for the special session and sets about constructing the legislative machinery that will force Tyler into compliance or irrelevance. The Fiscal Bank Bill is the instrument—designed not merely as policy but as a loyalty test. Tyler, increasingly isolated, searches for constitutional ground on which to resist while holding his cabinet together. Webster, caught between his genuine agreement with Tyler's constitutional position and his terror of being cast out by the Whig establishment, begins his double game—counseling Tyler privately while reassuring Clay's allies that compromise remains possible. The act traces the slow, grinding escalation from political pressure to political warfare: Clay's speeches grow more contemptuous, Tyler's resolve hardens, and Webster discovers that the middle ground he occupies is narrowing to a knife's edge. The turning point is Tyler's private decision—communicated to no one but rendered through his interior consciousness—that he will veto the bank bill, knowing it will bring the full weight of the Whig Party down upon him.
dramatic_beats: Clay's arrival in Washington and his first private meeting with Webster, where he lays out his legislative program with the serene confidence of a man who believes the presidency is his to direct
The special session convenes; Clay dominates the Senate floor and Tyler watches from the White House as his own party's leader treats the executive as a subordinate branch
Tyler's private meetings with Webster, where the two men debate the constitutionality of the bank bill—Webster torn, Tyler increasingly certain, the intellectual argument becoming a moral one
Ewing delivers the cabinet's collective recommendation that Tyler sign the bill, framing it as a matter of party survival; Tyler's silence is more devastating than any refusal
A dinner scene where Clay, in his cups, refers to Tyler as 'His Accidency' within earshot of men who will carry the phrase to the White House by morning
Tyler alone in the presidential study, reading the enrolled bill and composing his veto message—the act closes on his pen touching paper, the point of no return
acts.stakes_level: political and professional—careers, alliances, and the Whig legislative program of thirty years are now at hazard; the question has escalated from constitutional theory to the exercise of raw power

acts.title: The Veto and the Void
acts.description: Mid-August through September 1841. Tyler's veto of the Fiscal Bank Bill detonates the Whig coalition. The act opens with the veto message read aloud in the Senate—Clay's fury is volcanic, barely contained within the chamber's decorum. A hostile mob gathers outside the White House, burning Tyler in effigy, and for one night the presidency itself seems physically besieged. Clay orchestrates the coordinated cabinet resignations—every member but Webster submits his letter on the same day, a spectacle designed to humiliate Tyler into capitulation or resignation. Webster's decision to remain becomes the act's moral and dramatic center: he stays, and in staying he preserves both Tyler's administration and his own ambitions, but at a cost to his reputation that he will spend years attempting to repay. Tyler, stripped of his cabinet and his party, must reconstitute an administration from nothing. A second bank bill is introduced, modified to meet Tyler's stated objections; Tyler vetoes it again, proving that his resistance is not tactical but principled. The Whig caucus formally expels Tyler from the party. The act ends with Tyler alone in a White House emptied of allies, yet still President—the constitutional claim unbroken, the political wreckage total.
dramatic_beats: The veto message read in the Senate: Clay's face as the clerk intones Tyler's constitutional reasoning, and the eruption of partisan fury that follows
The mob outside the White House—burning effigies, shattered windows, Tyler's family inside hearing the crowd; the presidency reduced to a man standing in a besieged house
The coordinated cabinet resignations: Ewing's letter delivered first, then the others in rapid succession, each one a blade; Webster's conspicuous absence from the procession
Webster's private confrontation with Clay, who demands that Webster resign as well; Webster refuses, and the two men's friendship—already strained—fractures beyond repair
Tyler vetoes the second bank bill, destroying any remaining pretense that compromise is possible; the Whig caucus meets and formally reads Tyler out of the party
Tyler, alone in the executive mansion with Webster and a skeleton staff, dictates letters to prospective cabinet replacements—building a government from the ruins, his voice steady, his hands not
acts.stakes_level: national and existential—the question is no longer whether Tyler will survive politically but whether the constitutional office of the presidency can withstand a coordinated assault by the legislative branch and the president's own party

acts.title: The Precedent
acts.description: Late 1841 into early 1842. The wreckage has settled, and the novel's final act is concerned with consequences—political, constitutional, and personal. Tyler governs without a party, sustained by veto power and the sheer inertia of the executive office. Clay, his legislative program in ruins, begins the impeachment effort that will ultimately fail, and the failure completes his defeat: Congress cannot remove a president for disagreement, and the attempt only reinforces Tyler's constitutional position. Webster negotiates the opening moves of what will become the Webster-Ashburton Treaty, the one unambiguous achievement of the administration, and in doing so secures his own legacy while quietly distancing himself from Tyler's political isolation. The three POV characters arrive at their final positions: Tyler has won the constitutional argument and lost everything else; Clay has lost the constitutional argument and begun the long reckoning with his own methods; Webster has preserved his career and his principles, but the gap between them has become visible to everyone, including himself. The novel closes with Tyler receiving a diplomatic dispatch addressed to 'The President of the United States'—the title no longer contested, the precedent silently established, the man behind it already fading into the obscurity that is the price of his victory.
dramatic_beats: Clay introduces the impeachment resolution in the House; Tyler receives the news with a calm that unnerves even Webster, who expected rage
The House debate on impeachment: speeches that rehearse every argument of the crisis, ending in failure—the votes are not there, and the constitutional question is settled by congressional impotence
Webster's first diplomatic meeting with Lord Ashburton's advance party, establishing the treaty negotiation that will justify his decision to remain in office
A final private encounter between Tyler and Clay—two Virginians, two constitutional visions, two men who understand that the argument between them will outlast both their lives

Webster's quiet departure from Tyler's inner circle as the treaty work advances and his own presidential calculations resume—the alliance dissolving not in anger but in the simple arithmetic of ambition
The closing scene: Tyler alone in the presidential office, a dispatch addressed without qualification to the President of the United States; he reads it, sets it down, and the precedent is complete
acts.stakes_level: historical and existential—the stakes have transcended the personal and the political; what is being decided is the constitutional architecture of the American presidency for every successor who will occupy the office

chapter_outlines.title: The Cabinet Without a Head
chapter_outlines.setting: Harrison's sickroom and the Cabinet Room, the White House, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: President Harrison has been ill for weeks; the cabinet has governed in his absence with increasing autonomy. No president has ever died in office. The Constitution's succession clause is ambiguous—does the Vice President become President, or merely discharge presidential duties?
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Establish the constitutional void created by Harrison's death, introduce Webster's calculating intelligence, and dramatize the cabinet's immediate confusion over Tyler's status.
chapter_outlines.conflict: The cabinet members cannot agree on whether Tyler is now President or Acting President, and Webster must navigate the argument while positioning himself advantageously in whichever outcome prevails.
chapter_outlines.turn: Webster, reading the succession clause aloud to the assembled cabinet, realizes that the text is genuinely ambiguous—he had always assumed the answer was clear, and it is not. The constitutional ground is softer than anyone in the room has admitted.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The cabinet dispatches Fletcher Webster and a State Department clerk to Williamsburg to notify Tyler, but the letter they carry is carefully worded to avoid the word 'President'—a deliberate ambiguity that will enrage Tyler when he reads it. Webster has positioned himself as the constitutional authority in the room, but his authority rests on a question he cannot answer.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Webster discovers that Harrison, in his final lucid hours, told his physician that Tyler was 'a man of no consequence'—a private judgment that will shape the cabinet's posture toward the Vice President.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Ewing, whom Webster expected to defer to his constitutional reading, instead argues forcefully that Tyler is merely Acting President and that the cabinet should continue to govern collectively—revealing that Ewing has his own power base and will not be managed.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Ewing drafts a memorandum to Tyler asserting that Harrison's cabinet will continue to operate under the collective-governance model Harrison had promised, presenting it to Webster as a fait accompli before the messenger departs.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster notices that Ewing has taken the chair at the head of the cabinet table—the President's chair—and is irritated not by the presumption but by the fact that Ewing sat in it before Webster thought to do so himself.

chapter_outlines.title: The Horseman from Williamsburg
chapter_outlines.setting: Tyler's plantation home in Williamsburg, Virginia, and the road north toward Washington
chapter_outlines.political_context: Tyler is at home in Virginia, effectively sidelined from the Harrison administration. He has received no communication from the cabinet in weeks. The death notice arrives by messenger at dawn—Tyler learns he is either President or something no one has a name for.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize Tyler's private reckoning with the enormity of the claim he intends to make, establish his character through action and interior thought, and set him physically in motion toward Washington.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler must decide, before he reaches Washington, whether he will accept the diminished role the cabinet's carefully worded letter implies or claim the full presidency—knowing that the latter will make him enemies of the very men he needs to govern.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler reads the cabinet's notification letter and notices the studied absence of the word 'President.' He understands instantly that the omission is deliberate, and his resolve crystallizes: he will take the full oath or none at all.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Tyler departs Williamsburg within hours, traveling light and fast. He has committed himself to a constitutional position that no Vice President has ever claimed, and he has done so before consulting a single ally. His wife Letitia, confined to her chair by a stroke, watches him leave with an expression he cannot read.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Tyler discovers from the messenger that the cabinet has already begun issuing executive orders under their own collective authority—they are not waiting for him, and the longer he delays, the more deeply the 'Acting President' interpretation will entrench itself.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler had expected his old political ally Henry Wise to be waiting with counsel; instead Wise is in Washington already, and Tyler must make this decision entirely alone—the solitude he will later project as strength is, in this moment, terrifying.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: The messenger delivers not only the death notice but Ewing's memorandum on collective cabinet governance, which Tyler reads in his study—the cabinet's first attempt to define his role before he can define it himself.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler notices that the messenger's horse is finer than any in his own stable and resents, briefly and without irony, that a State Department clerk rides better than a Virginia gentleman.

chapter_outlines.title: The Oath Before Breakfast
chapter_outlines.setting: Brown's Indian Queen Hotel and the Circuit Court chambers, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: Tyler has arrived in Washington at dawn after a hard ride. The city is draped in mourning for Harrison. No formal arrangements have been made for a swearing-in because the cabinet has not conceded that one is necessary. Tyler must act before the political establishment can organize against his claim.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize the swearing-in as an act of constitutional audacity—Tyler seizing the initiative before his opponents can frame the question—and establish the oath as the foundational act of the entire novel.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler must find a judge willing to administer the full presidential oath on constitutionally untested grounds, in a city where the political establishment has not agreed that such an oath is warranted.
chapter_outlines.turn: Chief Justice William Cranch of the Circuit Court, after reading the succession clause and hesitating visibly, administers the oath—but adds a qualifying preamble that Tyler is taking the oath 'as doubts may arise,' subtly undercutting the absoluteness of Tyler's claim even as he enables it.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Tyler is sworn in, but the qualifying language gives his opponents a foothold: they can argue the oath was precautionary, not constitutive. Tyler, aware of this, immediately begins drafting his proclamation of full presidential authority. The constitutional battle is joined.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Webster arrives at the hotel before the oath and privately tells Tyler that the cabinet is divided—some members are prepared to accept him as President if he agrees to the collective-governance model, but others will resist any claim to independent executive authority. Tyler now knows the cabinet is not united against him but is not united for him either.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler expected hostility from Washington's political class; instead, several Democratic senators send private notes of congratulation, sensing an opportunity to fracture the Whig coalition—support from an unexpected quarter that is also, Tyler recognizes, entirely self-interested.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Mangum, as President pro tempore of the Senate, sends a formal note to Tyler addressing him as 'Vice President, acting as President'—the first official document to use the diminished title, establishing a competing interpretation before Tyler's proclamation can circulate.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler is pleased that his handwriting on the oath document is steadier than Judge Cranch's, and he notices this with a satisfaction that has nothing to do with constitutional principle.

chapter_outlines.title: The Proclamation
chapter_outlines.setting: The State Department offices and Tyler's temporary quarters at Brown's Hotel, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: Tyler has taken the oath but the political class remains divided on his status. Newspapers are beginning to weigh in, and the phrase 'Acting President' appears in several Whig organs. Tyler is drafting his proclamation of full authority. The cabinet has not yet formally met under Tyler's leadership.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Show Webster's calculation as he positions himself between Tyler and the cabinet, and dramatize the proclamation's drafting as an act of constitutional self-creation.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Webster must decide whether to help Tyler draft the proclamation—thereby endorsing the full-presidency claim—or to maintain studied neutrality. His choice will define his relationship with both Tyler and Clay for the remainder of the crisis.
chapter_outlines.turn: Webster reads Tyler's draft proclamation and realizes it is constitutionally sound—more than sound, it is brilliant in its simplicity. Webster had expected to find legal weaknesses he could exploit later; instead he finds an argument he genuinely believes, and this belief will trap him.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Webster offers minor editorial suggestions that Tyler accepts, making Webster a co-author of the constitutional claim he will later need to defend. Tyler issues the proclamation on April 9th. The document forces every political actor in Washington to take a position.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Webster learns through a private letter from a Kentucky correspondent that Clay has already begun drafting his legislative program for the special session—a program that assumes Tyler will be compliant. Clay is not reacting to the crisis; he has been planning for it.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Webster expected the proclamation to provoke immediate congressional resistance; instead, the initial reaction is muted—most senators and representatives are still in their home states, and the silence is more unsettling than opposition would have been.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Ewing visits Webster at the State Department and argues that Webster's assistance with the proclamation has compromised his standing with the cabinet, warning that Clay will view it as a betrayal—Ewing is drawing lines before the battle has formally begun.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster spends an unnecessary moment correcting a Latinate construction in Tyler's draft—not because it is wrong but because Webster's version is more elegant, and he wants Tyler to know it.

chapter_outlines.title: The President's Chair
chapter_outlines.setting: The Cabinet Room, the White House, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: Tyler's proclamation has been published. Newspapers are divided. The cabinet has been retained but has not yet met under Tyler's authority. The question of whether Tyler will govern independently or submit to collective cabinet rule remains unresolved. Harrison's funeral has taken place; the mourning period is ending, and business must resume.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize Tyler's first cabinet meeting as the moment where he establishes, through silence and a single devastating statement, that he will not be governed by the cabinet—planting the seeds of the later revolt.
chapter_outlines.conflict: The cabinet expects Tyler to ratify the collective-governance model Harrison had promised. Tyler intends to assert sole executive authority without provoking immediate resignations—he needs these men, but he will not be ruled by them.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler listens to each cabinet member's report without interruption, asks no questions, and closes the meeting with a single sentence: 'I can never consent to being dictated to as to what I shall or shall not do. I, as President, shall be responsible for my administration.' The room falls silent. The collective-governance model is dead.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The cabinet is stunned but does not resign—yet. Ewing and the others recognize that Tyler has drawn a line, but they believe Clay's arrival and the special session will force Tyler to retreat. Webster, alone among them, suspects Tyler will not retreat and begins planning accordingly.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: During the meeting, Tyler produces a letter from Harrison's files showing that Harrison himself had privately expressed doubts about the collective-governance model—the promise Clay's allies have been invoking was more conditional than they claimed.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler had expected Webster to support the collective-governance model as a way to increase cabinet influence; instead Webster remains silent throughout, offering Tyler neither support nor opposition—a neutrality that Tyler finds more alarming than hostility.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Ewing, immediately after the meeting, drafts a private letter to Clay in Kentucky reporting Tyler's statement verbatim and urging Clay to accelerate his plans for the special session—the cabinet revolt begins not with a confrontation but with a letter.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler notices that the cabinet members have rearranged the furniture in the Cabinet Room during Harrison's illness, moving the President's chair to a less prominent position. He has it moved back before anyone sits down, and he watches them notice.

chapter_outlines.title: The Summons from Ashland
chapter_outlines.setting: Ashland, Clay's estate near Lexington, Kentucky, and the road east toward Washington
chapter_outlines.political_context: Clay has received Ewing's letter reporting Tyler's assertion of full executive independence. Clay's legislative program—national bank, tariff, distribution—has been thirty years in the making, and the special session Harrison called before his death is the vehicle. Clay had expected a compliant president; he now faces a defiant one. He begins to marshal his forces.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Introduce Clay's perspective, establish his supreme confidence and the depth of his ambition, and dramatize his decision to come to Washington personally to bring Tyler to heel—ending Act 1 with the gathering menace of Clay's approach.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Clay must decide whether to manage Tyler from a distance through intermediaries or to come to Washington himself and risk a personal confrontation that could fracture the party. His pride demands the latter; his political instinct warns against it.
chapter_outlines.turn: Clay receives a second letter—this one from Mangum—reporting that Tyler has begun returning correspondence addressed to 'Acting President' unopened. Clay realizes that Tyler's defiance is not posturing but conviction, and that the man will have to be broken, not persuaded.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Clay departs for Washington with his legislative program fully drafted and his Senate allies instructed. He has committed himself to a direct confrontation with Tyler, and his confidence—though genuine—is built on the assumption that no president can survive without his party. The assumption will be tested.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Clay discovers from Mangum's letter that several Southern Whig senators have privately expressed sympathy for Tyler's states'-rights position on the bank question—the Whig majority is not as solid as Clay believed, and the fissure runs along the same sectional line that will eventually destroy the party.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Clay had planned to send his son to Washington as an advance emissary; instead his son declines, citing private business—a small domestic rebellion that mirrors the larger political one Clay is about to face, and that stings more than Clay will admit.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: A letter from Tyler arrives at Ashland—formal, courteous, and utterly unyielding—inviting Clay to consult on the legislative program but making clear that the executive will exercise independent judgment on all bills. Clay reads it aloud to his wife with a contempt that does not entirely conceal his unease.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Clay is irritated that the National Intelligencer's account of Tyler's proclamation is printed in larger type than the paper used for Clay's own Senate speeches, and he mentions this to his secretary with a sharpness that has nothing to do with typography.

chapter_outlines.title: The Lion Arrives
chapter_outlines.setting: Gadsby's National Hotel and the Capitol, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The special session of the 27th Congress is about to convene. Clay has arrived in Washington and established himself at Gadsby's Hotel, where a stream of Whig senators and representatives pay court. Tyler remains in the White House, increasingly aware that the legislative branch is being organized against him. Webster occupies the middle ground—Secretary of State, constitutionally loyal to Tyler, politically aligned with Clay's economic program.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize Clay's arrival and his first private meeting with Webster, establishing the Whig legislative program and Clay's serene confidence that Tyler will capitulate—while revealing the first cracks in Webster's double game.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Webster must navigate his first direct conversation with Clay since the succession crisis began, maintaining his position as Tyler's Secretary of State while not alienating the man who controls the Whig Party's future.
chapter_outlines.turn: Clay, in their private meeting, reveals that the bank bill has been drafted to be maximally provocative—not as a compromise but as a test of Tyler's submission. Webster realizes that Clay does not want Tyler's cooperation; he wants Tyler's surrender.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Webster leaves the meeting knowing that compromise is not Clay's objective. He must now decide whether to warn Tyler—and risk Clay's wrath—or to let events unfold and position himself to survive the wreckage. He chooses, for now, to say nothing, and the omission will haunt him.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Clay tells Webster privately that he has secured commitments from enough Whig senators to override a veto—or believes he has. Webster, who has been counting votes himself, knows Clay is three votes short but does not correct him.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Webster expected Clay to be angry about his assistance with Tyler's proclamation; instead Clay is magnanimous, almost dismissive—treating the proclamation as a minor embarrassment that the legislative session will render irrelevant. The condescension is worse than anger.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Clay hands Webster a printed copy of his legislative resolutions—bank, tariff, distribution—and asks Webster to deliver them to Tyler personally, making Webster the instrument of Clay's challenge to the President.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster notices that Clay's hotel suite is larger and better appointed than his own State Department office, and he dwells on this comparison for a moment longer than the observation warrants.

chapter_outlines.title: The Gavel Falls
chapter_outlines.setting: The Senate Chamber, United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The special session of the 27th Congress convenes. Clay commands the Whig majority in the Senate and intends to use it to drive his legislative program through both chambers before Tyler can organize resistance. The bank bill is the centerpiece, but tariff revision and distribution of land revenues are also on the docket. Tyler has no legislative allies of consequence.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Show Clay at the height of his power—dominating the Senate floor, orchestrating the legislative calendar, and treating the presidency as a subordinate institution—while introducing the Democratic opposition's quiet strategy of exploiting the Whig fracture.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Clay must maintain Whig unity while pushing legislation he knows Tyler opposes, managing both the Southern Whig defectors who share Tyler's constitutional scruples and the Northern Whigs who want even more aggressive economic nationalism.
chapter_outlines.turn: Silas Wright rises on the Senate floor and, in a speech of devastating precision, argues that Clay's bank bill is unconstitutional—using the same states'-rights reasoning Tyler has privately articulated. Clay realizes that the Democrats will provide Tyler with constitutional cover for a veto, transforming a party dispute into a constitutional confrontation.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Clay's legislative program is no longer a simple party matter—it is now entangled with the succession question and the constitutional debate over executive power. The Southern Whig senators who heard Wright's speech are visibly uncomfortable, and Clay must spend political capital he had not expected to expend.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Mangum privately informs Clay that Tyler has been meeting with Democratic senators—not to form an alliance but to gather constitutional arguments against the bank bill. Tyler is arming himself, and Clay's assumption that Tyler is intellectually isolated is wrong.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Clay expected the special session to open with a display of Whig unity; instead, the first day's proceedings reveal procedural disagreements among his own allies about the order of business, forcing Clay to spend the opening hours managing his coalition rather than attacking Tyler.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Wright delivers his speech on the Senate floor, providing the constitutional framework Tyler will later use in his veto message—the Democratic opposition is acting on-page, not through back channels.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Clay is annoyed that Mangum's opening remarks as President pro tempore were longer than protocol required, consuming time Clay had reserved for his own resolutions, and he makes a point of checking his watch ostentatiously during Mangum's peroration.

chapter_outlines.title: The Secretary's Dilemma
chapter_outlines.setting: The State Department offices and Webster's private residence, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The special session is underway. Clay's bank bill is advancing through committee. Tyler has begun private consultations on the constitutionality of the bill but has not yet committed to a veto. Webster is caught between his role as Tyler's chief cabinet officer and his awareness that his political future depends on the Whig establishment Clay controls.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Deepen Webster's internal conflict by dramatizing his private meetings with Tyler on the bank bill's constitutionality—showing Webster torn between genuine constitutional conviction and raw self-interest.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler asks Webster directly for his constitutional opinion on the bank bill. Webster believes the bill is constitutionally defensible but politically unwise in its current form. Giving Tyler an honest answer will either alienate Clay or compromise Tyler's trust—possibly both.
chapter_outlines.turn: Webster, in a private meeting with Tyler, offers a carefully hedged opinion: the bill is constitutional but could be improved. Tyler sees through the hedging and asks Webster point-blank whether he would sign it if he were President. Webster cannot answer, and his silence tells Tyler everything.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Tyler now knows that Webster will not be a reliable constitutional ally on the bank question—Webster's convictions are real but subordinate to his calculations. Tyler begins to rely more heavily on his own judgment and less on cabinet counsel. The isolation that will define his presidency deepens.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Webster receives a private communication from Lord Palmerston's office indicating that Britain is prepared to negotiate the Maine boundary dispute—the diplomatic opportunity that will become the Webster-Ashburton Treaty. Webster now has a concrete, personal reason to remain in office regardless of the bank crisis.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Webster expected Tyler to be grateful for any constitutional support, however qualified; instead Tyler is coldly polite, and Webster realizes that Tyler has begun to see him not as an ally but as another man with his own interests—which is, of course, exactly what Webster is.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Ewing visits Webster and presents a draft letter from the cabinet collectively urging Tyler to sign the bank bill, asking Webster to add his signature. Webster must decide on the spot whether to sign—joining the cabinet's pressure campaign—or refuse and further isolate himself.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster is quietly gratified that the British communication was addressed to him personally rather than to the President, and he reads his own name on the document with a pleasure that has nothing to do with diplomacy.

chapter_outlines.title: The Dinner at Gadsby's
chapter_outlines.setting: Gadsby's National Hotel, private dining room, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The bank bill is moving through Congress. Tyler has given no public indication of his intentions. Clay's confidence remains high, but the Southern Whig defection is a growing concern. Washington's social season—such as it is during a special session—provides the setting for political maneuver disguised as hospitality.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize the dinner scene where Clay, in his cups, refers to Tyler as 'His Accidency'—the phrase that will define Tyler's presidency in the public imagination—and reveal Clay's contempt as both a political weapon and a personal failing.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Clay must maintain the appearance of party unity at a social gathering while privately managing dissent among his Senate allies, several of whom are present and wavering on the bank bill.
chapter_outlines.turn: Clay, emboldened by wine and the flattery of his companions, refers to Tyler as 'His Accidency' in a voice loud enough to carry beyond the private dining room. The phrase is overheard by a correspondent for the Madisonian, Tyler's unofficial organ. Clay knows instantly that the remark will reach the White House, and he does not care—or rather, he is glad.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The phrase 'His Accidency' enters the political vocabulary within days, appearing in newspapers across the country. Tyler receives it as a personal insult that hardens his resolve. Clay has won a rhetorical victory that costs him a political one—Tyler will now veto the bank bill not merely on constitutional grounds but with the cold fury of a man who has been publicly humiliated.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: At the dinner, a Southern Whig senator—John C. Calhoun's ally—privately tells Clay that Calhoun has been corresponding with Tyler about states' rights and the bank question. Clay's enemy is advising Clay's nominal ally, and the ideological realignment that will eventually destroy the Whig Party is already underway.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Clay expected the dinner to consolidate Whig unity; instead, two wavering senators leave early, and their departure is noted by everyone in the room—the social gesture of leaving a Clay dinner early is, in Washington, a political statement.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: The Madisonian correspondent, present at the dinner under the guise of social reporting, takes careful notes of Clay's remarks and departs to file his account—the press as an active instrument of political warfare, operating in real time.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Clay notices that the wine served at dinner is inferior to what he serves at Ashland and mentions this to his host with a smile that does not reach his eyes—a small cruelty disguised as connoisseurship.

chapter_outlines.title: The Weight of the Enrolled Bill
chapter_outlines.setting: The President's study, the White House, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The Fiscal Bank Bill has passed both chambers of Congress. Tyler has ten days to sign or veto. The cabinet is pressing him to sign. Clay's allies are confident. Webster has offered qualified constitutional support for the bill but has not urged Tyler to sign it. Tyler is alone with the decision.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize Tyler's private reckoning with the veto decision—the constitutional reasoning, the political cost, and the personal terror of defying his own party—culminating in his decision to veto.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler must weigh his constitutional convictions against the near-certainty that a veto will destroy his political standing, provoke cabinet resignations, and possibly lead to impeachment. Every advisor urges him to sign. His conscience demands refusal.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler, reading the enrolled bill in his study late at night, finds a clause that would give the bank's directors authority to establish branches in states without those states' consent—a provision that violates his deepest constitutional convictions about state sovereignty. The clause is not new; Tyler has read it before. But in this moment, alone, with the weight of the decision upon him, the clause becomes the fixed point around which his refusal crystallizes.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Tyler begins drafting his veto message. He has crossed the point of no return. The veto will be the first open rupture between a president and his own party's congressional leadership, and Tyler knows that the consequences will be severe, personal, and irreversible.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Tyler discovers, in reviewing the bill's legislative history, that Clay personally inserted the offending clause after a compromise version had been informally agreed upon with Tyler's allies—Clay sabotaged the compromise deliberately, preferring confrontation to accommodation.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler had expected his wife Letitia to counsel caution; instead, through her companion, she sends word that she trusts his judgment—a rare communication from a woman largely confined by illness, and one that Tyler receives as both comfort and burden.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Ewing arrives at the White House unannounced and delivers the cabinet's formal, collective recommendation that Tyler sign the bill, framing refusal as a betrayal of the party that elevated him—the pressure campaign reaches its climax in Tyler's own study.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler notices that Ewing's boots have tracked mud onto the study carpet and is annoyed by this more than by Ewing's political arguments—a small, irrational irritation that he does not suppress.

chapter_outlines.title: The Knife's Edge
chapter_outlines.setting: Webster's private residence and the State Department, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: Tyler has privately decided to veto the bank bill but has not yet announced his decision. Webster suspects the veto is coming but has not been told directly. He is simultaneously managing the opening diplomatic exchanges with Britain on the Maine boundary and calculating how to survive the political explosion the veto will trigger.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Show Webster's double game reaching its most precarious point—counseling moderation to both sides while securing his diplomatic position as insurance against the coming storm.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Webster must prepare for two contradictory outcomes: if Tyler signs, Webster must be seen as having counseled wisely; if Tyler vetoes, Webster must be positioned to survive the cabinet revolt without joining it.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler summons Webster to the White House and reads him the draft veto message. Webster, hearing the constitutional argument laid out in Tyler's measured Virginia cadences, recognizes that the argument is not merely defensible but may be right—and that his own hedging has been a form of cowardice.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Webster is now the only cabinet member who knows the veto is coming. He does not warn Clay or Ewing. His silence is both a betrayal of his Whig allies and a commitment to Tyler's constitutional position—though Webster would not describe it in either of those terms. He would call it prudence.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Tyler tells Webster that he has received intelligence suggesting Clay has already drafted the cabinet resignation letters—the resignations are not a spontaneous response to the veto but a premeditated political operation. The veto and the resignations are both scripted; only the outcome is uncertain.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Webster expected to feel relieved at being trusted with the veto decision in advance; instead he feels trapped—Tyler's confidence is a chain, not a gift, and Webster cannot now claim ignorance of what is about to happen.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Clay sends a personal note to Webster—warm, collegial, assuming the bill will be signed—that functions as both a social courtesy and a political threat: if the bill is vetoed, Clay's warmth will turn to ice, and Webster will be on the wrong side of the most powerful man in the Whig Party.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster rereads Clay's note and is irritated by Clay's handwriting, which is bold and careless—the hand of a man who assumes his words will be preserved regardless of their presentation. Webster's own hand is meticulous, and he considers this a mark of superior character.

chapter_outlines.title: The Pen Touches Paper
chapter_outlines.setting: The President's study, the White House, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The ten-day signing period is expiring. Tyler has completed his veto message. The cabinet expects him to sign. Clay expects him to sign. The Whig press expects him to sign. Tyler will not sign.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize the final hours before the veto—Tyler's last opportunity to retreat—and close Act 2 with the irrevocable act of Tyler signing the veto message, the point of no return.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler faces a final wave of pressure: personal appeals from cabinet members, warnings from Whig allies, and his own awareness that the veto will make him the most isolated president in American history. Against all of this stands his reading of the Constitution and his understanding of what the presidency must be.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler signs the veto message and hands it to his secretary for delivery to Congress. The act is quiet—no witnesses but a clerk, no ceremony, no drama. The drama is in what follows.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The veto message will be read in the Senate the following day. Tyler has irrevocably broken with Clay, with his cabinet, and with his party. The constitutional crisis that has been building since April 4th is now open warfare. Act 2 closes on the most consequential signature of Tyler's presidency.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Tyler's secretary informs him that a delegation of Whig congressmen had been waiting in the anteroom to make a final personal appeal—and that Tyler's instructions to admit no visitors had turned them away without Tyler knowing they were there. The isolation is now physical as well as political.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler had steeled himself for anguish at the moment of signing; instead he feels a strange calm—the decision, once made, is lighter than the deliberation that preceded it. The terror he expected does not arrive, and its absence is itself unsettling.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Ewing makes one final attempt, arriving at the White House door and demanding to see the President. Tyler's secretary refuses him entry on Tyler's standing orders. Ewing stands in the hallway for a full minute before turning away—the cabinet's last chance to prevent the veto, lost to a closed door.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler, after signing the veto message, notices that the pen he used is the same one Harrison used to sign his inaugural address, and he is pleased by this continuity in a way that has nothing to do with constitutional principle and everything to do with a Virginian's sense of propriety.

chapter_outlines.title: The Clerk Reads
chapter_outlines.setting: The Senate Chamber, United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: Tyler's veto message arrives in the Senate. The Whig majority had expected the bill to be signed. Clay has spent months building the legislative machinery for his American System. The veto is a direct repudiation of everything Clay has worked for, and he receives it as both a political catastrophe and a personal insult.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize the veto message being read aloud in the Senate—Clay's reaction as the constitutional reasoning unfolds, the eruption of partisan fury, and the beginning of the end of the Whig coalition.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Clay must decide in real time how to respond to the veto: with measured constitutional argument, which would dignify Tyler's position, or with open fury, which would satisfy his rage but risk alienating moderate Whigs who might be needed for an override attempt.
chapter_outlines.turn: As the clerk reads the veto message, Clay realizes that Tyler's constitutional argument mirrors the states'-rights reasoning Clay himself deployed against Andrew Jackson's bank veto in 1832—Tyler is using Clay's own weapons against him. The irony is devastating, and Clay's fury becomes volcanic.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Clay rises and delivers an extemporaneous speech denouncing the veto in terms that stop just short of calling Tyler a usurper. The speech electrifies the Whig caucus but fails to secure the votes for an override—the Southern Whigs will not cross the states'-rights line. Clay's legislative program is dead, and he knows it before the day is over.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: During the Senate debate, a Democratic senator reads into the record a private letter from Clay to Harrison, written before Harrison's inauguration, in which Clay explicitly states that the President should defer to congressional will on economic legislation—proving that Clay's vision of the presidency has always been one of executive subordination, not partnership.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Clay expected the Whig caucus to unite in outrage; instead, several Southern Whig senators sit in conspicuous silence during his speech, and their silence is louder than any dissent.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Silas Wright rises after Clay's speech and delivers a brief, devastating response: the veto power exists precisely for moments like this, and the Senate's fury is evidence not of Tyler's illegitimacy but of the Constitution working as designed. Wright's speech reframes the debate on Tyler's terms.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Clay notices that the Senate clerk reading the veto message stumbles over a word and must restart a sentence, and Clay is irrationally pleased by this—as though the clerk's incompetence somehow diminishes the message itself.

chapter_outlines.title: The Bonfire on the Lawn
chapter_outlines.setting: The White House, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The veto has been sustained—the Senate cannot muster the two-thirds majority for an override. But the political explosion is immediate. A crowd gathers outside the White House, initially Whig celebrants who arrived expecting the bill to be signed. When the veto becomes known, the celebration turns hostile. Tyler is burned in effigy on the White House lawn.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize the mob scene outside the White House as the physical manifestation of Tyler's political isolation—the presidency reduced to a man standing in a besieged house—and show Tyler's response as a mixture of fear, defiance, and the stubborn composure that defines his character.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler must decide whether to respond to the mob—to appear, to speak, to send for the militia—or to endure the humiliation in silence. His family is inside the White House, hearing the crowd. The presidency has never been physically threatened by the president's own party.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler's son Robert urges him to appear at a window and address the crowd. Tyler refuses—not from cowardice but from a constitutional conviction that the President does not answer to mobs. The refusal is the same stubbornness that produced the veto, applied to a different arena, and it costs Tyler something his son can see but Tyler will not acknowledge.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The mob disperses by dawn, but the damage is done. Tyler's effigy has been burned in front of the executive mansion, and the image will circulate in newspapers for weeks. Tyler's family is shaken. The social isolation that will characterize the remainder of his presidency begins here—invitations withdrawn, old friends suddenly unavailable, the White House emptied of visitors.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Tyler's secretary reports that several members of the mob were identified as congressional staffers—the demonstration was not spontaneous but organized, at least in part, by Clay's allies in the Capitol. The line between political opposition and personal intimidation has been crossed.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler expected the mob to frighten him; instead, hearing them chant 'His Accidency' outside his windows, he feels not fear but a cold clarity—they have given him the name he will wear, and he discovers he can bear it.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: A group of Whig congressmen arrive at the White House gate during the demonstration and demand to see the President, claiming they wish to mediate; Tyler's secretary recognizes them as Clay's lieutenants and turns them away, but their presence at the gate during the mob scene is itself a form of pressure.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler notices that the effigy the mob burned was dressed in a coat finer than any he actually owns, and he is briefly, absurdly offended by the inaccuracy of the costume.

chapter_outlines.title: The Letters
chapter_outlines.setting: The State Department offices and the White House, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The first veto has been sustained. Clay is orchestrating the cabinet resignations. A revised bank bill—the Fiscal Corporation Bill—is being drafted, ostensibly to meet Tyler's stated objections. Webster knows the resignations are coming and must decide whether to join them or remain.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize the period between the two vetoes as Webster navigates the impossible space between Tyler and Clay, and show the revised bank bill as Clay's final trap—designed to make Tyler's second veto look like obstinacy rather than principle.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Webster must determine whether the revised bill genuinely addresses Tyler's constitutional objections or is a cosmetic rewrite designed to make the second veto politically untenable. His answer will determine whether he stays or goes.
chapter_outlines.turn: Webster obtains a copy of the revised bill and discovers that while the language has been softened, the operative provisions are substantively identical to the original—the revision is a fraud, designed to give Clay the political argument that Tyler is unreasonable. Webster now knows that Clay's goal is not legislation but destruction.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Webster begins to prepare his position for remaining in the cabinet after the resignations. He drafts a private memorandum justifying his decision on constitutional and diplomatic grounds—the Webster-Ashburton negotiations provide the perfect pretext. But the memorandum is also a confession: Webster is staying because he wants to stay, and the reasons are as much about ambition as about principle.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Webster learns from Ewing—who tells him almost casually—that the resignation letters have already been written and are being held for coordinated delivery on the day of the second veto. The resignations are not a response to the veto; they are timed to it, a theatrical production with Clay as director.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Webster expected Ewing to pressure him to resign; instead Ewing tells him, with unexpected gentleness, that Webster should do what he thinks best—a moment of human decency from a man who is otherwise executing Clay's plan with mechanical precision.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Clay sends an emissary to Webster with a direct message: resign with the cabinet or be permanently excluded from the Whig Party. The threat is delivered in person, in Webster's office, by a man Webster has known for twenty years.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster, reading Ewing's resignation letter in draft, notices that Ewing's prose style is clumsy and is privately pleased that his own public statement, when it comes, will be better written—a satisfaction that has nothing to do with constitutional principle.

chapter_outlines.title: The Second Veto
chapter_outlines.setting: The President's study and the Cabinet Room, the White House, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The Fiscal Corporation Bill has passed both chambers. Tyler's allies had privately signaled he might accept a genuine compromise, but Tyler has concluded—with Webster's quiet confirmation—that the revised bill is substantively identical to the first. The second veto will destroy every remaining bridge to the Whig Party.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize Tyler's second veto as the definitive act of defiance—not a repetition of the first but a deepening of it, proving that his resistance is principled rather than tactical.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler must veto a bill that has been publicly presented as a compromise addressing his stated objections. The political cost of the second veto is even greater than the first—he will be portrayed as a man who cannot be satisfied, who vetoes not from principle but from obstinacy.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler signs the second veto message and, for the first time, adds a personal postscript to his secretary: 'Let them do their worst.' The postscript is not included in the official message, but it marks the moment Tyler stops defending his position and begins to embrace his isolation.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The second veto triggers the coordinated cabinet resignations within hours. Tyler is now a president without a cabinet, without a party, and without allies in either chamber of Congress. The constitutional crisis has reached its maximum intensity.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Tyler receives word, within an hour of signing the veto, that Ewing has already submitted his resignation letter—proving that the resignation was prepared before the veto was even signed. Tyler now has documentary proof that the cabinet revolt was premeditated, not reactive.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler expected to feel the same calm he felt after the first veto; instead the second veto fills him with a grief he did not anticipate—not for the political consequences but for the friendships he is about to lose. The grief passes quickly, but it was real.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Ewing delivers his resignation letter to Tyler in person, reading it aloud in the Cabinet Room—a calculated humiliation, forcing Tyler to sit and listen as his own Secretary of the Treasury denounces his constitutional judgment.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler notices that Ewing's resignation letter is dated the day before the veto—a clerical error, perhaps, but Tyler chooses to interpret it as proof of premeditation, and the interpretation gives him a satisfaction that is more personal than political.

chapter_outlines.title: The Procession of Letters
chapter_outlines.setting: The White House and the State Department, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The coordinated cabinet resignations are underway. Ewing has resigned. Bell, Badger, Crittenden, and Granger submit their letters in rapid succession over the course of a single day—a spectacle designed to humiliate Tyler and signal to the country that his administration is ungovernable. Webster alone has not resigned.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize the mass resignation as a political spectacle and Webster's decision to remain as the act's moral and dramatic center—showing his refusal as simultaneously principled, self-interested, and courageous.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Webster must refuse to resign while every other cabinet member is doing so, knowing that his refusal will be interpreted as either heroic loyalty or craven ambition—and that both interpretations contain truth.
chapter_outlines.turn: Webster, after watching the fourth resignation letter arrive, walks to the White House and tells Tyler directly: 'I am still here, Mr. President.' Tyler's response—'Give me your hand upon it, Mr. Webster'—is the only moment of genuine warmth between the two men in the entire novel, and it is over in seconds.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Webster's refusal to resign preserves Tyler's administration—without a Secretary of State, Tyler would have no functioning cabinet at all. But Webster's decision also isolates him from the Whig establishment. Clay's response, when it comes, will be swift and personal. Webster has chosen his side, and the choice is irrevocable.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Webster learns that Granger, the Postmaster General, hesitated before resigning—he was prepared to stay if Webster stayed, but Clay's emissary reached him first. Webster could have saved a second cabinet member if he had acted sooner, and the knowledge that he did not is a private wound.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Webster expected Tyler to be grateful, even effusive; instead Tyler receives his declaration with a formal courtesy that borders on coldness. Tyler trusts Webster's decision but not Webster's motives, and the distinction is clear to both men.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: The fifth and final resignation letter—Granger's—arrives while Webster is in Tyler's office, delivered by a clerk who does not know Webster is present. The timing is accidental, but the effect is theatrical: Webster watches the last of his colleagues depart while he stands in the room he has chosen not to leave.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster notices that Badger's resignation letter contains a grammatical error and feels a flash of contempt for a man who cannot even resign properly—a judgment that has nothing to do with politics and everything to do with Webster's estimation of his own literary superiority.

chapter_outlines.title: The Fracture
chapter_outlines.setting: Clay's suite at Gadsby's Hotel and the Senate Chamber, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The cabinet resignations have been executed. Tyler is isolated. Clay moves to complete the destruction by having the Whig caucus formally expel Tyler from the party—an act without precedent, designed to strip Tyler of any remaining political legitimacy.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize the Whig caucus expulsion of Tyler and Clay's private confrontation with Webster, showing Clay at the peak of his destructive power and the moment where his fury begins to curdle into something less controlled.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Clay must manage the expulsion vote while confronting Webster's refusal to resign—a refusal that undermines the unanimity of the cabinet revolt and gives Tyler a lifeline Clay did not intend to leave.
chapter_outlines.turn: Clay confronts Webster privately and demands his resignation. Webster refuses, citing the ongoing negotiations with Britain and his constitutional duty. Clay, for the first time in the novel, loses his composure entirely—not in a dramatic eruption but in a cold, precise dismantling of Webster's stated reasons, exposing the ambition beneath the principle. The two men's friendship fractures beyond repair.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The Whig caucus formally expels Tyler from the party. Tyler is now a president without a party—the first in American history. Clay has won every tactical battle and cannot understand why he feels as though he is losing. Webster, shaken by Clay's attack, retreats into the diplomatic work that will justify his decision.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: During the confrontation, Clay reveals that he knows about the British diplomatic communication Webster received weeks ago—the Webster-Ashburton opportunity. Clay accuses Webster of staying not for constitutional reasons but to secure a personal diplomatic triumph, and Webster cannot deny it because it is partly true.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Clay expected the expulsion vote to be unanimous; instead, three Southern Whig senators abstain, and their abstention is reported in the press as a sign that Tyler's constitutional position has support even within the party that has expelled him.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Webster, during the confrontation with Clay, produces Tyler's proclamation of April 9th and reads a passage aloud—the constitutional argument for full presidential authority—forcing Clay to engage with the legal substance of Tyler's claim rather than dismissing it as pretension.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Clay, after Webster leaves, notices that Webster left his gloves on the chair and is irrationally annoyed—not by the oversight but by the presumption that Webster will return to retrieve them, as though the conversation were merely interrupted rather than concluded.

chapter_outlines.title: The Empty Rooms
chapter_outlines.setting: The White House, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: Tyler has been expelled from the Whig Party. His cabinet has resigned en masse except for Webster. He must reconstitute an administration from nothing while governing a country whose legislative branch is controlled by his enemies. The White House staff has been reduced; social invitations have ceased. Tyler is the most isolated president in American history.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Show Tyler in the immediate aftermath of the cabinet revolt—rebuilding his government from the ruins, dictating letters to prospective replacements, and confronting the practical reality of governing without institutional support.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler must find qualified men willing to serve in a cabinet that the Whig Senate will almost certainly refuse to confirm. Every appointment is a political battle, and Tyler's pool of candidates is limited to men who have nothing to lose—or who want something Tyler can give them.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler's first cabinet nominee is rejected by the Senate within hours of submission—a humiliation designed to demonstrate that Tyler cannot staff his own government. Tyler, rather than retreating, submits a second name immediately, signaling that he will outlast the Senate's capacity for rejection.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Tyler begins the slow, grinding process of rebuilding his administration. The Senate's obstructionism proves that the constitutional crisis is not over—it has merely shifted from the veto power to the appointment power. But Tyler's persistence reveals a stubbornness that even his enemies must respect.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Tyler discovers that one of his rejected nominees was privately warned by Clay's allies not to accept the nomination—the Senate is not merely rejecting nominees but intimidating potential candidates before they can be named. Tyler's isolation is being actively manufactured.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler expected Webster to help with the cabinet reconstruction; instead Webster is increasingly absorbed in the British negotiations and offers only perfunctory assistance—the alliance is already beginning to dissolve, not in anger but in the quiet arithmetic of diverging interests.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Mangum, as President pro tempore, orchestrates the rapid rejection of Tyler's first nominee on the Senate floor, delivering a brief speech that frames the rejection as a defense of the Senate's prerogatives rather than a partisan attack—the institutional language disguising the partisan motive.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler, reviewing the list of potential cabinet nominees, crosses off a name not because the man is unqualified but because he once kept Tyler waiting in an anteroom for forty-five minutes in 1837, and Tyler has not forgotten.

chapter_outlines.title: The Impeachment Gambit
chapter_outlines.setting: The Capitol—House of Representatives gallery and Clay's private office, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: Tyler is slowly rebuilding his cabinet with non-Whig appointments, some of which the Senate grudgingly confirms. Clay, frustrated by the failure of his legislative program and the resilience of Tyler's constitutional position, turns to the ultimate weapon: impeachment. A resolution is introduced in the House.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize Clay's decision to pursue impeachment and the House debate that follows—showing the attempt as both the logical culmination of Clay's campaign and the moment where his methods begin to exceed his judgment.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Clay must persuade the House Whig caucus to support impeachment without appearing to be pursuing a personal vendetta. The constitutional grounds are weak—Tyler has exercised the veto power, which is his unambiguous right—and Clay knows that failure will strengthen Tyler's position.
chapter_outlines.turn: During the House debate, a Whig congressman whom Clay considered a reliable ally rises and argues against impeachment—not on Tyler's behalf but on constitutional grounds, warning that impeaching a president for exercising the veto would destroy the separation of powers. Clay, watching from the gallery, realizes that the impeachment will fail and that its failure will be his most consequential defeat.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The impeachment resolution is tabled—the votes are not there. Clay's campaign against Tyler has reached its limit. The constitutional question is effectively settled: a president cannot be removed for policy disagreements, and Tyler's claim to the full presidency is reinforced by Congress's inability to challenge it.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Clay learns from a House ally that several Whig representatives voted against impeachment not out of constitutional conviction but because they fear the precedent—if Tyler can be impeached for vetoing legislation, so can any future president, including a future Whig president. Clay's weapon has been turned against him by his own party's self-interest.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Clay expected the impeachment debate to energize the Whig base; instead, public opinion in several Northern states shifts slightly toward Tyler—the spectacle of a party trying to remove its own president strikes many voters as excessive, and the Whig press begins to distance itself from Clay's position.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: The Whig congressman who speaks against impeachment does so on the House floor, in Clay's presence, using arguments that echo Tyler's own constitutional reasoning—the opposition comes from within Clay's coalition, not from outside it.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Clay, watching the debate from the gallery, notices that the congressman who spoke against impeachment is wearing a new coat—finer than Clay's own—and wonders, with a pettiness he does not bother to examine, who is paying for it.

chapter_outlines.title: The Envoy
chapter_outlines.setting: The State Department and a private residence on Lafayette Square, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The impeachment effort has failed. Tyler's constitutional position is effectively secure, though his political isolation continues. Webster has begun formal diplomatic exchanges with Lord Ashburton's advance party regarding the Maine boundary dispute. The treaty negotiations are Webster's justification for remaining in office and his path to a legacy independent of the Tyler crisis.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize Webster's first substantive diplomatic meeting with the British advance party, establishing the treaty negotiation as the one unambiguous achievement of the administration—and showing Webster's quiet distancing from Tyler as his own presidential calculations resume.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Webster must conduct sensitive diplomatic negotiations while serving a president whom the British regard as constitutionally dubious. The British envoy's first question—whether Tyler's government will survive long enough to ratify a treaty—forces Webster to defend a president he is already beginning to leave behind.
chapter_outlines.turn: The British envoy, in a private aside, tells Webster that Lord Ashburton will negotiate only if he is assured that Tyler is President in the full constitutional sense—not an acting officer whose agreements might be repudiated. Webster, to secure the negotiation, must make the strongest possible case for Tyler's legitimacy, and in doing so he commits himself more deeply to Tyler's constitutional position than he has ever done publicly.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Webster's defense of Tyler's legitimacy to the British becomes, paradoxically, the strongest public endorsement of the Tyler precedent—a foreign power's recognition that Tyler is President carries a weight that domestic political arguments cannot match. Webster has served Tyler's cause while serving his own, and the entanglement of motives is complete.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: The British envoy privately discloses that Palmerston's government has been receiving intelligence from Clay's allies suggesting that Tyler's government is about to collapse—Clay has been undermining Tyler's diplomatic position abroad as well as at home, a revelation that shocks even Webster.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Webster expected the British to be difficult negotiating partners; instead they are eager—the Maine boundary dispute is a genuine threat to Anglo-American peace, and the British want resolution as much as Webster does. The negotiation will be easier than Webster anticipated, which means his justification for remaining in office may expire sooner than he planned.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: The British envoy presents Webster with a memorandum questioning Tyler's constitutional authority to negotiate treaties, forcing Webster to respond in writing—the opposing move comes not from Clay but from the international stage, raising the stakes of the legitimacy question beyond domestic politics.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster is annoyed that the British envoy's French is better than his own and compensates by deploying an unnecessarily complex Latin legal phrase in his response, which the envoy does not understand—a small linguistic victory that pleases Webster more than it should.

chapter_outlines.title: Two Virginians
chapter_outlines.setting: The White House, a private parlor, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The impeachment has failed. Tyler's cabinet is slowly being reconstituted. The constitutional question is effectively settled in practice if not in formal law—Tyler is addressed as President, his vetoes are sustained, and his administration functions. But the political wreckage is total: Tyler has no party, few allies, and a hostile Congress. Clay's legislative program is dead.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize a final private encounter between Tyler and Clay—two Virginians, two constitutional visions—in which both men confront the permanence of what has happened and the argument between them that will outlast both their lives.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Tyler and Clay meet privately—ostensibly to discuss a minor legislative matter, but in reality to take the measure of each other one last time. The conflict is no longer tactical but existential: each man believes the other has damaged the republic, and neither can entirely dismiss the other's argument.
chapter_outlines.turn: Clay, in a moment of unguarded honesty, tells Tyler that history will judge them both—and that Clay is no longer certain which of them will be judged more harshly. It is the closest Clay will ever come to acknowledging that Tyler may have been right, and Tyler recognizes it as such without pressing the advantage.
chapter_outlines.consequences: The meeting changes nothing politically—Clay will continue to oppose Tyler, and Tyler will continue to govern without him. But the encounter humanizes both men and establishes the novel's central theme: that constitutional arguments are never merely abstract but are fought by men with pride, ambition, and the capacity for self-doubt.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Clay admits, obliquely, that he inserted the offending clause in the original bank bill deliberately—not to provoke a veto but to test whether Tyler would submit. The admission confirms what Tyler suspected but could not prove, and it transforms Clay's legislative strategy from policy disagreement into a calculated assault on presidential independence.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Tyler expected to feel triumph in Clay's presence; instead he feels something closer to pity—Clay is visibly aged by the crisis, and Tyler recognizes in Clay's face the same exhaustion he sees in his own mirror. The victory, such as it is, tastes of nothing.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: Clay, even in this private meeting, cannot resist making one final political move: he asks Tyler to support a modified tariff bill as a gesture of goodwill, knowing Tyler will refuse and hoping to use the refusal in future speeches. Tyler sees the maneuver and declines without anger.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Tyler notices that Clay's cravat is tied in the old Virginia fashion—the same style Tyler's father wore—and is irrationally annoyed that Clay, of all men, should remind him of his father.

chapter_outlines.title: The Distance Between Them
chapter_outlines.setting: The State Department and Webster's private study, Washington, D.C.
chapter_outlines.political_context: The crisis has passed its acute phase. Tyler governs as an independent president. Webster's treaty negotiations are advancing. The Whig Party is fractured but functional. The constitutional precedent—that the Vice President becomes President upon the incumbent's death—is settling into accepted practice, reinforced by every document, every diplomatic exchange, every act of governance that treats Tyler as President without qualification.
chapter_outlines.chapter_goal: Dramatize Webster's quiet departure from Tyler's inner circle as the treaty work advances and his own presidential calculations resume—the alliance dissolving not in anger but in the simple arithmetic of ambition.
chapter_outlines.conflict: Webster must begin distancing himself from Tyler without appearing disloyal—he needs the treaty to be completed under his name, but he also needs to rebuild his standing with the Whig establishment for a future presidential run. The two objectives are increasingly incompatible.
chapter_outlines.turn: Tyler, in a private meeting, thanks Webster for his loyalty and then adds, with a precision that cuts: 'I trust you will remain as long as it serves the nation's interest—and your own.' Tyler has seen through Webster's calculations, and the acknowledgment, delivered without malice, is more devastating than any accusation.
chapter_outlines.consequences: Webster and Tyler's relationship enters its final phase—professional, courteous, and emptied of the intimacy that the crisis briefly created. Webster will complete the treaty and depart. Tyler will govern alone. The alliance has served its purpose for both men, and both men know it.
chapter_outlines.hard_reveal: Webster learns that Tyler has begun private conversations with Democratic leaders about a potential third-party presidential run in 1844—Tyler is not merely surviving but planning a political future that does not include Webster. The revelation forces Webster to accelerate his own departure.
chapter_outlines.soft_reversal: Webster expected his distancing from Tyler to go unnoticed; instead Tyler facilitates it—assigning Webster's diplomatic duties to subordinates, reducing their private meetings, making the separation mutual. Tyler is managing Webster's departure as carefully as Webster is planning it.
chapter_outlines.on_page_opposing_move: A Whig newspaper publishes an editorial—clearly inspired by Clay's allies—questioning Webster's loyalty and suggesting that his continued service under Tyler disqualifies him from future Whig leadership. The editorial is the Whig establishment's warning shot, delivered through the press.
chapter_outlines.petty_moment: Webster, packing papers in his study, comes across a draft of Tyler's April proclamation with his own editorial suggestions in the margins. He considers keeping it as a historical document, then puts it back—but not before noting, with a satisfaction he does not examine, that his suggested revisions were the best sentences in the final text.

chapter_outlines.title: The Precedent
chapter_outlines.setting: The President's office, the White House, Washington, D.C.
