scenes.immediate_risk: If Clay's speech fails to unify his caucus, the first day of the special session will be remembered as the moment the Whig majority revealed its fractures to the nation's press, who fill the gallery.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: The newspaper correspondents in the gallery will report Whig disunity, setting the public perception for the entire session and giving Tyler political cover to resist.
scenes.power_shift_target: Clay—this is his moment of maximum rhetorical dominance, and the scene should feel like a high-water mark.
scenes.turn: As Clay reaches his peroration, invoking the mandate of Harrison's election, he catches Silas Wright exchanging a glance and a barely perceptible nod with Senator Rives across the aisle—a silent communication that suggests the Democrats are already in contact with the wavering Whigs.
scenes.consequences: Clay's speech is publicly triumphant but privately alarming. He has seen the enemy's hand. The press will report a commanding performance, but Clay now knows the Democrats are not merely opposing—they are recruiting.
scenes.emotional_beat: Exhilaration curdling into apprehension—the orator's high dissolving into the strategist's dread.
scenes.continuity_notes: Clay's speech references Harrison's inaugural promises and the Whig platform of 1840. Mangum presides from the chair. The gallery includes newspaper correspondents from the National Intelligencer, the Madisonian, and the Globe. Rives is seated but does not speak. Wright is present and observing. Time is approximately 11:30 AM.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Mangum, presiding, must maintain the appearance of impartiality from the chair while being Clay's instrument—his rigid posture and refusal to meet Clay's eyes during the speech suggest the strain of this dual role.
scenes.opponent_actor: The Democratic caucus as a body, with Silas Wright as its visible representative
scenes.opponent_move: Wright exchanges a coordinated signal with Rives during Clay's speech, demonstrating that the Democrats have already established communication with wavering Southern Whigs.
scenes.pov_countermove: Clay extends his peroration by two minutes, directly addressing the Southern Whigs by invoking party loyalty and the memory of Harrison's mandate, attempting to shame potential defectors before the gallery.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: The press correspondents note the Southern Whigs' visible discomfort and the Wright-Rives exchange, and the next morning's papers lead with Whig division rather than Whig strength.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The opening day's proceedings are being recorded by correspondents whose dispatches must reach their papers by evening post.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Clay grips the edge of his desk with both hands as he sits down after his speech, knuckles whitening, though his face remains composed in a senator's mask of satisfaction.
scenes.physical_interruption: Mid-speech, Clay's voice catches on a dry throat and he must pause to sip water from the glass on his desk—a silence of three seconds that feels, to him, like an eternity under the gallery's gaze.

scenes.setting: A private committee room off the Senate corridor, sparsely furnished with a long oak table, straight-backed chairs, and a single window overlooking the Capitol grounds. Early afternoon, 31 May 1841. The session has recessed for dinner.
scenes.goal: Clay summons Thomas Ewing to coordinate the cabinet's pressure on Tyler—he wants Ewing to deliver a message to the President that the bank bill will pass and that a veto will be treated as a declaration of war upon the Whig Party.
scenes.opposition: Ewing, while loyal to Clay's program, is reluctant to deliver so blunt a threat to Tyler, fearing it will harden the President's resistance rather than secure his compliance. Ewing also carries intelligence that Webster is privately counseling Tyler to seek a compromise—meaning the cabinet is not as unified as Clay assumed.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Clay pushes Ewing too hard, the Secretary of the Treasury may soften the message or refuse to deliver it, and Clay will have revealed his strategy to a man who might share it with Webster.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: If Ewing reveals Clay's ultimatum strategy to Webster, Webster will warn Tyler, and the President will have time to prepare his constitutional defense before the bill reaches his desk.
scenes.power_shift_target: Ewing—he enters the scene as Clay's subordinate and leaves with leverage, because he possesses information Clay needs about Webster's disloyalty.
scenes.turn: Ewing reveals that Webster visited Tyler at the White House two days prior and advised the President that a modified bank bill—one with state opt-out provisions—might be constitutionally acceptable. Clay realizes that Webster is running a parallel negotiation that could undermine the pure Whig bill and give Tyler an escape route.
scenes.consequences: Clay must now fight on two fronts: against Tyler's potential veto and against Webster's compromise effort within the cabinet. He resolves to accelerate the bank bill through committee to deny Webster time to broker a deal.
scenes.emotional_beat: Betrayal—the cold recognition that an ally is operating independently, and the ruthless recalculation that follows.
scenes.continuity_notes: Ewing is Secretary of the Treasury, not Secretary of State—Webster holds that office. Webster's visit to Tyler occurred two days prior (29 May). The bank bill is still in committee; it has not yet reached the floor for debate. Clay's threat to Tyler has not yet been delivered. Time is approximately 1:00 PM.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Clay wants Ewing to serve as his messenger, delivering an unambiguous threat to Tyler that a veto will end the President's relationship with the Whig Party.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Ewing wants to preserve his own position by remaining useful to Clay without becoming the instrument of a rupture with Tyler that could destroy the cabinet—and he wants Clay to know about Webster's freelancing, both as a warning and as a bid for Clay's trust over Webster.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Clay conceals his growing suspicion that the bank bill may not pass without amendment—he presents it to Ewing as a certainty to maintain the pressure.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Ewing conceals the full extent of his own conversations with Webster—he has not merely heard about Webster's visit to Tyler; he was present for part of the discussion and tacitly acquiesced to the compromise proposal before thinking better of it.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Clay begins in command, issuing orders. When Ewing reveals Webster's independent negotiation, Clay is forced to ask questions rather than give directives—Ewing becomes the man with intelligence Clay desperately needs.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: This is a scene about the limits of Clay's control over his own coalition—the moment he discovers that commanding the Senate floor is not the same as commanding the government.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Webster's unseen maneuvering with Tyler creates a second axis of opposition that Ewing introduces into the scene, forcing Clay to reassess his entire strategy.
scenes.opponent_actor: Thomas Ewing
scenes.opponent_move: Reveals that Webster has visited Tyler and proposed a compromise bank bill with state opt-out provisions, undermining Clay's strategy of forcing Tyler to accept or reject the pure Whig bill.
scenes.pov_countermove: Clay instructs Ewing to carry the original unmodified threat to Tyler and orders him to cease all communication with Webster regarding the bank bill until Clay authorizes it.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Ewing leaves the meeting uncommitted and reports the conversation to Webster, who accelerates his compromise negotiations with Tyler before Clay can counter.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The Finance Committee meets tomorrow morning to advance the bank bill; Clay must have his strategy settled before then.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Clay pulls a chair out from the table and does not sit in it—stands behind it with his hands on its back, as though holding a barrier between himself and Ewing.
scenes.physical_interruption: A splinter from the rough underside of the oak table catches under Clay's thumbnail as he leans forward, drawing a bead of blood he must press against his trouser leg while maintaining his composure.

scenes.setting: The Senate Chamber, United States Capitol—afternoon session reconvened, the galleries still crowded but the light now slanting through the western windows. Late afternoon, 31 May 1841.
scenes.goal: Clay intends to manage the afternoon's procedural business—committee assignments, calendar motions—to ensure the bank bill moves to the floor on his timetable, before Webster's compromise can gain traction.
scenes.opposition: Silas Wright rises to deliver a speech on the constitutionality of the proposed national bank, deploying states'-rights arguments with devastating precision—arguments that mirror Tyler's known private objections.
scenes.immediate_risk: Wright's speech, delivered to a full gallery and a full chamber, provides constitutional cover for any Whig senator who wishes to vote against the bank bill. Every word Wright speaks makes it easier for Rives and the Southern Whigs to defect.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: If Clay cannot effectively answer Wright on the floor, the next day's newspapers will frame the bank bill as constitutionally contested rather than as settled Whig policy—a framing that transforms a party vote into a constitutional crisis and gives Tyler the public justification for a veto.
scenes.power_shift_target: Silas Wright—he enters the scene as a member of the minority opposition and leaves having reframed the entire debate on terms favorable to Tyler and the Democrats.
scenes.turn: Wright, in the climax of his speech, quotes directly from Tyler's 1833 Senate speech opposing the recharter of the Second Bank of the United States—demonstrating that the President's constitutional position is not new, not improvised, and not subject to party discipline. Clay realizes the Democrats will provide Tyler with intellectual armor against the Whig majority, transforming a party dispute into a constitutional confrontation that Clay cannot win by votes alone.
scenes.consequences: The bank bill is no longer a simple test of party loyalty—it is now a constitutional question that engages the deepest anxieties of the Southern Whig senators. Clay must spend political capital to hold his majority together. The session's first day, which Clay intended as a demonstration of Whig power, has instead revealed the fault lines that will define the entire Congress.
scenes.emotional_beat: Dread—the recognition that the ground has shifted beneath his feet, that the battle he prepared for is not the battle he will fight.
scenes.continuity_notes: Wright quotes Tyler's 1833 Senate speech—this is a real historical document. Rives is visibly moved by the speech. Mangum, presiding, cannot intervene. The gallery correspondents are writing furiously. The bank bill is still in committee but scheduled for floor debate within the week. Time is approximately 4:00 PM. Clay has not yet responded publicly to Wright's arguments.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Wright wants to reframe the bank bill debate from a party-loyalty question into a constitutional question, providing Tyler and wavering Southern Whigs with intellectual and legal cover to oppose Clay.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Clay wants to contain Wright's speech as a partisan performance—to dismiss it as Democratic obstructionism rather than engage with its constitutional substance.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Wright conceals the extent of his coordination with Tyler's allies—this speech was not spontaneous but was prepared in consultation with Democrats who have been in contact with the White House.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Clay conceals his knowledge of Tyler's letter to Rives and of Webster's compromise negotiations—he cannot reveal these without exposing the depth of Whig division.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Clay begins the afternoon session in procedural command. Wright's speech systematically dismantles that command by shifting the debate from procedure to principle—terrain where Clay's majority is irrelevant. By the speech's end, Wright holds the intellectual initiative.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: This is a scene about the limits of legislative power when confronted with constitutional argument—the moment Clay discovers that controlling the votes is not the same as controlling the meaning of those votes.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Mangum, presiding from the chair, must rule on procedural points during Wright's speech and is visibly torn between his duty as presiding officer and his loyalty to Clay—his hesitation on a ruling about speaking time gives Wright an additional two minutes that prove decisive.
scenes.opponent_actor: Silas Wright Jr.
scenes.opponent_move: Rises on the Senate floor and delivers a prepared constitutional argument against the bank bill, quoting Tyler's own 1833 Senate speech to demonstrate the President's long-held and principled opposition.
scenes.pov_countermove: Clay rises on a point of order to challenge the relevance of Wright's historical citations, then delivers an extemporaneous rebuttal arguing that the election of 1840 constituted a popular mandate for the bank—attempting to shift the ground from constitutional law to democratic legitimacy.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Wright's speech goes unanswered on the floor, and the evening newspapers report it as the definitive constitutional case against the bank bill, with no Whig counter-argument on record.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The Senate will adjourn for the day within ninety minutes; Clay must answer Wright before the correspondents file their evening dispatches.
scenes.required_end_hook: Wright's speech has provided Tyler with constitutional armor and the Southern Whigs with permission to defect—Clay must now decide whether to accelerate the bank bill to a vote before the opposition can consolidate, or to seek the compromise he has spent the day trying to prevent.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Clay sits motionless at his desk during Wright's peroration, his pen held above a sheet of paper as though about to write, but the nib never touches the page.
scenes.physical_interruption: The late-afternoon sun strikes Clay directly in the eyes through the western windows as he rises to respond, forcing him to shield his face with one hand for a moment before the angle shifts—a brief, undignified squint before the full Senate.

scenes.setting: The State Department offices, second floor, a cramped suite of rooms on Fifteenth Street near the Treasury — Webster's private office, heavy with the smell of old paper, sealing wax, and the June heat pressing through windows that will not open fully. A desk buried under diplomatic correspondence, a half-empty decanter of Madeira on the sideboard.
scenes.goal: Webster seeks to draft a confidential memorandum summarizing his constitutional position on the bank bill before his scheduled meeting with Tyler — to fix his own mind on a defensible middle ground that will satisfy both Clay's expectations and Tyler's constitutional scruples.
scenes.opposition: A messenger arrives from Henry Clay bearing a terse note demanding Webster's attendance at a private dinner that evening, with the implicit expectation that Webster will report on Tyler's disposition toward the bank bill — forcing Webster to confront the fact that Clay regards him as an informant, not an independent officer.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Webster is seen receiving Clay's note by his own clerks — one of whom is known to be friendly with the National Intelligencer's correspondents — the fact of Clay's back-channel communication with the Secretary of State will become public gossip before nightfall.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: Webster's reputation as an independent constitutional officer is permanently compromised if his role as Clay's intermediary becomes public knowledge; Tyler would never trust him again.
scenes.power_shift_target: Henry Clay — even in absentia, Clay's note forces Webster into a reactive posture, reminding him who controls the Whig machinery.
scenes.turn: Webster reads Clay's note, begins composing a politely evasive reply declining the dinner, then stops — realizing that declining will itself be read as a declaration of independence from Clay that he is not yet prepared to make. He tears up the draft reply and writes an acceptance instead.
scenes.consequences: Webster commits to attending Clay's dinner, knowing he must now decide before evening what intelligence he is willing to share about Tyler's thinking. His memorandum on the bank bill remains unfinished — his own constitutional position unsettled. He enters the Tyler meeting that afternoon without the intellectual armor he intended to prepare.
scenes.emotional_beat: Dread — the slow recognition that every path forward narrows.
scenes.continuity_notes: Clay's note is hand-delivered, not posted. Webster has not yet met with Tyler on the bank bill this session. The Madeira decanter is half-empty — Webster has been drinking more than usual. The clerk who might leak is named Derrick — establish him here for later use. Time: late morning, June 1841. Webster's memorandum is left unfinished on his desk.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Clay's note functions as a proxy for Clay's entire apparatus of expectation — the Whig caucus, the Senate committee structure, the patronage network — all pressing on Webster through a single folded sheet of paper.
scenes.opponent_actor: Henry Clay (via his messenger and written note)
scenes.opponent_move: Delivers a note summoning Webster to a private dinner with the implicit demand that Webster report on Tyler's constitutional disposition toward the bank bill.
scenes.pov_countermove: Begins drafting a refusal, then tears it up and writes an acceptance — choosing tactical compliance over premature defiance.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: The clerk Derrick, loitering near the door, observes the messenger's livery and the note's seal, and the fact of Clay's summons enters the Washington gossip circuit within hours.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: Clay's dinner is this evening; Webster must compose his reply and settle his own position before his afternoon meeting with Tyler.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Tears the draft reply in half, then in quarters, then aligns the pieces precisely before sweeping them into the waste-basket.
scenes.physical_interruption: A bead of sweat runs from his temple into his collar, and he cannot stop himself from pulling at the damp linen, losing his train of thought mid-sentence in the memorandum.

scenes.setting: The President's private study on the second floor of the Executive Mansion — a room still bearing the marks of Harrison's brief occupancy: a military map pinned to the wall that no one has removed, a writing desk repositioned to face the south windows. Tyler sits in a cane-backed chair, coat off, in the heat. Late afternoon light slants through half-drawn curtains.
scenes.goal: Webster intends to offer Tyler a carefully calibrated constitutional opinion on Clay's bank bill — one that acknowledges the bill's defensibility while steering Tyler toward requesting amendments, thereby buying time and preserving Webster's standing with both sides.
scenes.opposition: Tyler, who has been reading the bill's text and consulting Upshur and Tucker independently, presses Webster for an unambiguous answer — not a lawyer's hedge but a statesman's conviction. Tyler's directness strips away Webster's prepared equivocations.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Webster gives Tyler an unequivocal endorsement of the bill's constitutionality, Tyler will know Webster is Clay's man and will cease confiding in him. If Webster expresses doubt, word will reach Clay through Ewing before the dinner, and Clay will treat it as betrayal.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: Webster permanently loses Tyler's confidence as a constitutional counselor — the one role that gives him independent value in the administration and justifies his remaining in the cabinet when the break with Clay comes.
scenes.power_shift_target: John Tyler — he enters the scene as the supplicant seeking counsel but ends it holding the superior position, having exposed Webster's inability to commit.
scenes.turn: Tyler asks Webster point-blank: 'If you sat in this chair, Mr. Webster — if the bill lay before you with pen and ink — would you sign it?' Webster opens his mouth, hesitates, and says nothing. The silence lasts long enough for both men to understand its meaning. Tyler thanks him quietly and changes the subject to the Northeastern boundary dispute.
scenes.consequences: Tyler now understands that Webster's constitutional convictions, though genuine, will always be subordinate to his political calculations. Tyler begins to rely more heavily on his own judgment and on Virginia allies outside the cabinet. Webster leaves the meeting knowing he has failed a test he did not anticipate, and that Tyler's courtesy in changing the subject was itself a form of dismissal.
scenes.emotional_beat: Shame — the specific shame of a brilliant man caught in the machinery of his own cleverness.
scenes.continuity_notes: Tyler has been reading the bank bill text independently — he quotes specific provisions. He has consulted Upshur and Tucker (Virginia allies) but does not name them to Webster. The military map on the wall is Harrison's — a visual reminder of the dead president. Webster's memorandum is unfinished; he does not have his prepared notes. Tyler pivots to the Northeastern boundary dispute — this plants a seed for the Webster-Ashburton Treaty subplot. Time: late afternoon, same day as Scene 1.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Tyler wants an honest constitutional opinion from the one cabinet member whose legal mind he respects — but more than that, he wants to know whether Webster is his man or Clay's.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Webster wants to preserve his position with both Tyler and Clay by offering a nuanced opinion that commits him to neither side — to be the indispensable mediator.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Tyler has already consulted Upshur and Tucker and is forming his own view that the bill is unconstitutional in its current form; he is testing Webster, not genuinely seeking guidance.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Webster believes the bill is constitutionally defensible but politically catastrophic for Tyler, and he cannot say so without revealing that his primary concern is political survival, not constitutional principle.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Tyler begins as the man seeking counsel, apparently deferential to Webster's legal authority. The power shifts decisively when Tyler asks the direct question and Webster cannot answer — Tyler's silence-reading capacity proves superior to Webster's verbal dexterity.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: This is a loyalty test disguised as a constitutional consultation. Tyler is determining whether he is alone, and Webster is discovering that he cannot serve two masters without serving neither.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Thomas Ewing's known role as Clay's cabinet enforcer hovers over the conversation — both men are aware that anything Webster says may reach Ewing and thence Clay before morning.
scenes.opponent_actor: John Tyler
scenes.opponent_move: Asks Webster the direct question — 'If you sat in this chair, would you sign it?' — stripping away all diplomatic hedging and forcing a binary commitment.
scenes.pov_countermove: Attempts to reframe the question as hypothetical and therefore unanswerable, then falls silent when Tyler does not accept the reframe — the silence itself becomes the countermove, an involuntary confession.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Tyler concludes that Webster is Clay's agent within the cabinet and ceases sharing his constitutional deliberations with the Secretary of State, cutting Webster out of the most consequential decision of the administration.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: Webster must attend Clay's dinner this evening; whatever he says to Tyler now will determine what he can credibly report to Clay in a matter of hours.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Picks up a pen from Tyler's desk, turns it in his fingers as though about to write, then sets it down without having used it — a mime of the signing act Tyler has just described.
scenes.physical_interruption: A fly buzzes persistently near Webster's ear during the critical exchange, and he swats at it involuntarily, breaking the gravity of the moment in a way that embarrasses him.

scenes.setting: Webster's private residence on Louisiana Avenue — the parlor, evening. Heavy curtains drawn against the lingering June twilight. Webster sits alone with a glass of brandy before departing for Clay's dinner. His wife Caroline is upstairs; the house is quiet except for the sound of a servant setting out his evening clothes.
scenes.goal: Webster attempts to compose himself and decide what he will tell Clay about Tyler's disposition — seeking a formulation that is truthful enough to maintain Clay's trust but vague enough to preserve his own freedom of maneuver.
scenes.opposition: Webster's own conscience and the memory of Tyler's unanswered question — the internal recognition that he has already begun to betray both men by trying to serve both.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Webster cannot settle on a coherent position before arriving at Clay's dinner, his improvisation under Clay's direct questioning will expose his vacillation, and Clay — who is merciless with men he considers weak — will write him off as useless.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: Webster's self-conception as a man of principle — the Daniel Webster of the Reply to Hayne, the constitutional oracle — fractures permanently under the weight of his own equivocation.
scenes.power_shift_target: No external power shift — this is an interior reckoning. Webster's self-knowledge deepens, but his capacity for decisive action does not increase.
scenes.turn: Webster catches sight of himself in the parlor mirror — dressed for Clay's dinner, brandy in hand — and for one unguarded moment sees himself as Tyler must have seen him that afternoon: a man of immense gifts and insufficient courage. He finishes the brandy, stands, and resolves to tell Clay that Tyler is 'deliberating with care' — a formulation that conveys nothing and commits to nothing, the perfect sentence of a man who has chosen to remain in the middle.
scenes.consequences: Webster departs for Clay's dinner armed with a phrase designed to satisfy without informing. He has chosen strategic ambiguity over conviction — a choice that will define his role for the remainder of the crisis. The unfinished memorandum on his State Department desk becomes a symbol of his unfinished commitment.
scenes.emotional_beat: Self-contempt — the bitter clarity that comes from seeing oneself truly and choosing not to change.
scenes.continuity_notes: The brandy is from the same stock Webster keeps at home — not the Madeira at the office. Caroline is present in the house but does not appear on-page. The evening clothes are being laid out by a servant — Webster dresses formally for Clay's dinner, indicating its political seriousness. The unfinished memorandum remains at the State Department. Time: early evening, same day.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Caroline Webster's unseen presence upstairs — her awareness of her husband's political entanglements and her unspoken judgment — adds domestic weight to the scene without requiring her appearance.
scenes.opponent_actor: Self — specifically, the memory of Tyler's unanswered question and the image in the mirror.
scenes.opponent_move: The mirror reflection confronts Webster with the gap between his self-image as a constitutional statesman and his actual conduct as a political trimmer.
scenes.pov_countermove: Finishes the brandy, stands, and rehearses aloud the phrase 'deliberating with care' — choosing calculated ambiguity as his weapon for the evening.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Webster arrives at Clay's dinner without a prepared formulation and, under Clay's direct questioning, improvises badly — revealing either too much about Tyler's doubts or too little to maintain Clay's confidence.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: Clay's dinner begins within the hour; Webster must compose himself and depart.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Holds the empty brandy glass up to the lamplight and studies the residue as though reading an augury, then sets it down with deliberate care on the mantelpiece.
scenes.physical_interruption: His dress collar is too tight — the summer heat has swollen his neck — and he tugs at it repeatedly, unable to get comfortable, a petty indignity that undercuts his attempt at composure.

scenes.setting: Henry Clay's rented rooms near the Capitol — a well-appointed parlor converted into a dining room for the evening. A table set for six, though only Clay and Webster are present when the scene opens; the other guests have not yet arrived. Candles lit against the dusk, windows open to admit whatever breeze the Potomac might offer. The smell of roasted fowl from the kitchen.
scenes.goal: Webster aims to satisfy Clay's demand for intelligence on Tyler's bank bill disposition while revealing as little as possible — to maintain his value to Clay without becoming Clay's instrument against Tyler.
scenes.opposition: Clay, who is direct, relentless, and contemptuous of evasion, presses Webster for a clear reading of Tyler's intentions and demands that Webster use his influence to bring Tyler into line on the bank bill.
scenes.immediate_risk: Clay may interpret Webster's evasiveness as disloyalty and begin treating him as an obstacle rather than an ally — a shift that would trigger Clay's formidable capacity for political destruction.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: If Clay concludes that Webster is unreliable, he will route all cabinet coordination through Ewing and cut Webster out of Whig strategy entirely, leaving Webster isolated in a cabinet that serves Clay's purposes, not his own.
scenes.power_shift_target: Henry Clay — he enters with leverage (the Whig machinery, the Senate majority) and ends with even more, having extracted from Webster's careful evasions the confirmation that Tyler is not compliant.
scenes.turn: Clay, growing impatient with Webster's hedged phrases, sets down his glass and says flatly that if Tyler vetoes the bank bill, the cabinet must resign en masse — all of them. He watches Webster's face for a reaction. Webster, caught off guard, asks whether Clay means to include the Secretary of State in that number. Clay's answer — 'I mean to include every man who values his standing in this party' — transforms the dinner from a consultation into a threat.
scenes.consequences: Webster now knows that Clay is prepared to orchestrate a mass cabinet resignation to destroy Tyler politically, and that Webster's own position is conditional on compliance. The dinner ends with surface cordiality but the underlying relationship has shifted: Webster is no longer Clay's colleague but his hostage. Webster returns home understanding that the bank bill crisis will force him to choose — and that the choice, when it comes, will cost him something irreplaceable regardless of which side he takes.
scenes.emotional_beat: Entrapment — the sensation of walls closing in while maintaining the fiction of a pleasant evening among friends.
scenes.continuity_notes: Clay mentions the mass resignation plan for the first time in Webster's hearing — this is new intelligence for Webster. The other expected dinner guests have not arrived — their absence may be deliberate on Clay's part, ensuring a private confrontation. Clay references Ewing as already aligned with the resignation plan. Webster's phrase 'deliberating with care' is deployed and Clay sees through it immediately. The roasted fowl goes largely uneaten. Time: evening, same day as all previous scenes.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Clay wants confirmation that Tyler will not sign the bank bill in its current form, and he wants Webster's commitment to the mass resignation strategy as a weapon against Tyler.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Webster wants to maintain Clay's confidence while preserving his own freedom to remain in the cabinet — specifically, to protect his ability to negotiate the Northeastern boundary treaty, which requires continuity in office.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Clay has already discussed the resignation strategy with Ewing and possibly Crittenden; this dinner is not a consultation but a fait accompli being presented as a question.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Webster's real reason for wanting to stay in the cabinet — the Ashburton negotiation and his presidential ambitions — which would reveal that his loyalty to the Whig cause is entirely instrumental.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Clay begins with leverage and increases it steadily. Webster's attempt to deploy his prepared phrase ('deliberating with care') fails immediately; Clay's blunt escalation to the resignation threat leaves Webster with no rhetorical ground to stand on.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: This is an ultimatum disguised as a dinner invitation. Clay is not seeking Webster's counsel — he is demanding Webster's submission, and the intimacy of the setting (two men alone, food and wine) makes the coercion more personal and therefore more effective.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Thomas Ewing is invoked by Clay as already committed to the resignation plan — his absent agreement functions as institutional pressure, suggesting the cabinet is already moving without Webster's consent.
scenes.opponent_actor: Henry Clay
scenes.opponent_move: Declares that the entire cabinet must resign if Tyler vetoes the bank bill, and directly challenges Webster to confirm his participation in the collective action.
scenes.pov_countermove: Asks whether Clay means to include the Secretary of State — a deflection that attempts to make the threat specific and therefore negotiable, buying time without committing.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Clay interprets Webster's silence as refusal and begins planning the cabinet resignation without Webster's participation, effectively expelling him from Whig inner councils.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The bank bill is advancing through committee and will reach Tyler's desk within weeks; Clay frames the resignation plan as requiring immediate commitment.
scenes.required_end_hook: Clay's ultimatum — resign with the cabinet or be destroyed as a Whig — hangs unanswered as Webster departs into the Washington night, with Tyler's unanswered question from the afternoon echoing against Clay's unanswered demand, and the bank bill moving inexorably toward the President's desk.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Cuts a piece of the roasted fowl on his plate, raises it to his mouth, then sets the fork down without eating — repeating this gesture twice during Clay's ultimatum without appearing to notice.
scenes.physical_interruption: A candle gutters and spits hot wax onto the tablecloth near Webster's hand, and he flinches — a visible startle that Clay observes and that Webster cannot disguise as anything other than nerves.

scenes.setting: Clay's suite at Gadsby's National Hotel, second floor, the private parlour overlooking Pennsylvania Avenue—heavy curtains drawn against the late June heat, a writing desk cluttered with correspondence and Senate tally sheets, a decanter of Madeira already opened.
scenes.goal: Clay is reviewing the Senate vote count for the bank bill and preparing his strategy for the evening's dinner, at which he intends to shore up wavering Southern Whig senators by reminding them of party discipline and the rewards of loyalty.
scenes.opposition: A letter from Senator William C. Rives of Virginia, delivered that afternoon, expressing grave reservations about the bank bill's constitutionality and hinting that he will vote against it—the first formal defection among Clay's Virginia allies.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Clay cannot formulate a response to Rives before the dinner, Rives's defection will embolden other wavering Southerners who are attending tonight, and Clay will lose control of the evening's political choreography.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: Rives's letter, if left unanswered, becomes a template for other Southern Whigs to break ranks publicly before the bill reaches the floor.
scenes.power_shift_target: Clay begins with full confidence; by scene's end, Rives's letter has introduced a crack in his certainty that he papers over with contempt rather than strategy.
scenes.turn: Clay reads Rives's letter a second time and recognizes that Rives is not merely expressing personal doubt but channelling Tyler's constitutional objections almost verbatim—the President is working behind the scenes to peel away Southern votes.
scenes.consequences: Clay's suspicion that Tyler is actively undermining the bank bill hardens into conviction. His anger toward Tyler becomes personal, not merely political. He decides to use the dinner not just to rally allies but to humiliate Tyler's position through ridicule, setting the stage for the 'His Accidency' remark later in the evening.
scenes.emotional_beat: Controlled fury masquerading as aristocratic disdain.
scenes.continuity_notes: Rives's letter must be physically present—Clay folds it and places it in his coat pocket, where it remains for the rest of the chapter. The Madeira decanter is already one-third empty before the dinner begins. Time is late afternoon, approximately five o'clock. Clay is dressed for dinner but not yet wearing his coat. The tally sheet shows 28 firm yes, 4 wavering, 2 likely no—Rives now moves to likely no.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: William C. Rives's written defection forces Clay to recalculate his entire evening strategy; Rives is not present but his words act as Tyler's proxy.
scenes.opponent_actor: William C. Rives (as proxy for Tyler's behind-the-scenes lobbying)
scenes.opponent_move: Delivers a letter formally declaring constitutional objections to the bank bill, echoing Tyler's known position and signalling an organized Southern Whig defection.
scenes.pov_countermove: Drafts a terse reply to Rives demanding a private meeting before the Senate vote, then pockets the letter to use as evidence of Tyler's interference when addressing allies at dinner.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Other Southern Whig senators at dinner learn of Rives's defection through rumour rather than Clay's controlled framing, and the evening becomes a forum for open dissent rather than rallying.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: Dinner begins at seven o'clock; Clay has roughly two hours to formulate his response and reframe the evening's purpose.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Refills his glass of Madeira without looking at it, eyes fixed on Rives's handwriting as though the penmanship itself were the betrayal.
scenes.physical_interruption: A bead of sweat runs from his temple into his collar; the June heat in the closed room is suffocating, and he tugs irritably at his cravat before remembering he has not yet put on his coat.

scenes.setting: The main staircase and corridor of Gadsby's National Hotel, leading to the private dining room on the ground floor—gas lamps lit against the early dusk, the smell of roasting meat and tobacco smoke drifting from the public rooms, the murmur of other diners and lodgers.
scenes.goal: Clay descends to the dinner intending to take command of the room before the wavering senators can cluster together and reinforce each other's doubts. He wants to arrive first and arrange the seating to isolate the doubters among loyalists.
scenes.opposition: Senator John J. Crittenden of Kentucky, Clay's old friend and ally, intercepts him on the stairs with unwelcome news: Mangum has been heard telling colleagues that Tyler may be persuadable on the bank bill if certain concessions are made regarding state veto provisions—a compromise Clay considers a capitulation.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Mangum's compromise position gains traction among the dinner guests before Clay can discredit it, the evening will become a negotiation rather than a rallying, and Clay will lose the initiative.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: Mangum's compromise, once spoken aloud at dinner, becomes a viable alternative that moderate Whigs can rally around, permanently weakening Clay's all-or-nothing position on the bank.
scenes.power_shift_target: Clay retains dominance by the scene's end, but Crittenden's warning reveals that even loyalists are hedging.
scenes.turn: Crittenden, while warning Clay about Mangum, lets slip that he himself thinks some accommodation with Tyler might be prudent—revealing that Clay's closest ally is not fully committed to the confrontational strategy.
scenes.consequences: Clay realizes he cannot trust even Crittenden to hold the line absolutely. He resolves to make the dinner a demonstration of personal dominance rather than collegial persuasion—he will perform confidence so forcefully that no one dares raise the compromise. This performative mode, fuelled by wine, will lead directly to the fatal indiscretion.
scenes.emotional_beat: Betrayal laced with defiance—the loneliness of a man who discovers his allies are more cautious than he believed.
scenes.continuity_notes: Crittenden is wearing a dark coat and smells of cigar smoke. The staircase is narrow enough that the two men must speak close together, almost conspiratorially. Clay has Rives's letter in his coat pocket. Time is approximately quarter to seven. Mangum has not yet arrived at the dining room. Clay and Crittenden descend together but Clay enters the dining room first.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Crittenden wants to warn Clay about Mangum's compromise talk and gauge whether Clay might be willing to bend, thereby protecting Crittenden's own position if the party splits.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Clay wants to confirm Crittenden's absolute loyalty and use Crittenden as a lieutenant to suppress the compromise talk at dinner.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Crittenden hides his own growing belief that some accommodation with Tyler is the wiser course—he frames it as Mangum's position, not his own.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Clay hides the depth of his alarm at Rives's defection and his suspicion that Tyler is actively orchestrating opposition; he presents himself as fully in command.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Crittenden begins as a loyal messenger delivering intelligence; by the end, his inadvertent admission of sympathy for compromise shifts power toward uncertainty—Clay must now perform dominance rather than simply exercise it.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: Two old friends testing whether their alliance can survive a disagreement neither is willing to name directly—a negotiation of loyalty disguised as a tactical briefing on the stairs.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Willie Person Mangum's reported compromise position threatens to undercut Clay's strategy from within the party leadership itself—Mangum is not present but his words precede him.
scenes.opponent_actor: John J. Crittenden
scenes.opponent_move: Reveals Mangum's compromise position and then inadvertently admits his own sympathy for accommodation with Tyler, suggesting Clay's confrontational strategy may lack full support even among Kentucky allies.
scenes.pov_countermove: Dismisses the compromise as capitulation, invokes the party's mandate from the 1840 election, and instructs Crittenden to seat himself beside the most wavering senator at dinner to prevent private conversations about accommodation.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Crittenden enters the dining room uncommitted and gravitates toward the moderate faction, giving Mangum's compromise position an influential second voice before Clay can establish the evening's tone.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: Dinner guests are assembling now; Clay has minutes, not hours, to set the terms of the evening.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Clay places his hand on Crittenden's shoulder as they descend—a gesture that looks like warmth but is, in its pressure, a command to stop talking.
scenes.physical_interruption: Clay's knee, which has troubled him since the journey from Lexington, catches painfully on the narrow stair turn, forcing him to grip the banister and pause mid-sentence.

scenes.setting: The private dining room at Gadsby's National Hotel—a long table set for twelve, candles lit, heavy silver, the windows open to the avenue but admitting little relief from the heat. Senators, a few House members, and two Washington correspondents (one from the National Intelligencer, one from the Madisonian) are present. Wine flows freely.
scenes.goal: Clay dominates the dinner conversation, steering it away from compromise and toward a celebration of the bank bill's inevitability, using wit, charm, and the force of his personality to suppress dissent and bind the wavering senators to his position through social pressure.
scenes.opposition: Senator Mangum, seated midway down the table, raises the question of Tyler's likely response to the bill and suggests that certain modifications—particularly a state-veto provision—might secure the President's signature and avoid a constitutional confrontation.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Mangum's compromise proposal gains vocal support at the table, Clay's position as the unchallenged leader of the Whig legislative strategy collapses in front of witnesses, including two journalists.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: The journalists will report that the Whig caucus is divided, and the appearance of party unity—Clay's most powerful weapon—will be shattered in print within forty-eight hours.
scenes.power_shift_target: Clay dominates the table through the first half of dinner; Mangum briefly gains the room's attention with his compromise proposal; Clay recaptures dominance through ridicule, but at the cost of escalation that leads to the fatal remark.
scenes.turn: Mangum suggests that Tyler, as a man of Virginia and constitutional scruple, might sign a modified bill. Clay, now deep in his cups and riding the momentum of his own performance, responds with escalating mockery of Tyler's pretensions—culminating in the phrase 'His Accidency,' spoken loudly enough to carry to the adjoining room where the Madisonian correspondent has stepped out for air.
scenes.consequences: The phrase 'His Accidency' is overheard and will be reported. Clay has won the table—no one dares defend Tyler after such a display—but he has created a weapon that will be turned against him. The wavering senators are silenced, not persuaded; their compliance is temporary. Mangum withdraws into cold formality, his usefulness as a bridge to Tyler destroyed.
scenes.emotional_beat: Exhilaration curdling into recklessness—the intoxication of a man who mistakes dominance for victory.
scenes.continuity_notes: Mangum is seated at the middle of the table, not beside Clay. The Madisonian correspondent is identified as a young man named Fletcher—he has stepped into the corridor during the remark but the door is open. The National Intelligencer man is at the table and loyal to Clay. Rives is NOT present at this dinner. Clay has consumed at least four glasses of wine by this point. The candles are guttering in the heat. Crittenden is seated beside Senator Archer of Virginia, as Clay instructed. The time is approximately half past eight.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Clay wants to silence Mangum's compromise talk and demonstrate to every man at the table that Tyler is not a figure to be negotiated with but a political accident to be managed—he wants submission, not agreement.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Mangum wants to position himself as the reasonable broker between Clay and Tyler, preserving his own influence with both factions and preventing a veto that would damage the entire Whig project.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Clay hides his fear that the Southern defections are more serious than he has admitted, and that Tyler may have the constitutional and political ground to sustain a veto. His contempt for Tyler is partly a mask for this fear.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Mangum hides the extent of his private communications with Tyler's allies and his growing conviction that Clay's intransigence will destroy the party. He also conceals his awareness that the Madisonian correspondent is present.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Mangum holds the room's attention briefly when he raises the compromise; Clay seizes it back through wit and escalating mockery, culminating in the 'His Accidency' remark—but this is a pyrrhic seizure, as the remark will cost Clay more than the evening's dominance is worth.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: A succession struggle within the Whig Party itself—Clay and Mangum are not debating bank policy but fighting over who defines the party's relationship to executive power, and whether Clay or Tyler will be the true leader of the Whig coalition.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Mangum's compromise proposal directly challenges Clay's authority and forces Clay to choose between statesmanlike engagement and the performative dominance he has committed to. The Madisonian correspondent's presence—and his position near the open door—transforms a private indiscretion into a public event.
scenes.opponent_actor: Willie Person Mangum
scenes.opponent_move: Publicly proposes at the dinner table that Tyler might sign a modified bank bill with state-veto provisions, framing compromise as statesmanship and implicitly challenging Clay's confrontational strategy before the assembled senators and journalists.
scenes.pov_countermove: Ridicules Tyler's constitutional pretensions with escalating wit, culminating in the coinage 'His Accidency,' delivered with theatrical contempt that silences the table and forecloses further discussion of compromise.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Mangum's compromise proposal draws vocal support from two or three wavering senators at the table, and the Intelligencer correspondent notes the division for his next dispatch.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The dinner will break up within the hour; whatever impression is established at this table will be the version that reaches the press and the Senate cloakroom by morning.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Clay sets down his wine glass with deliberate care after delivering the 'His Accidency' line—the steadiness of his hand a performance of control that contradicts the recklessness of the words.
scenes.physical_interruption: A bone from the roast catches between Clay's teeth mid-sentence during an earlier toast, forcing him to pause and work it free with his tongue before resuming—a moment of undignified mortality in the midst of his rhetorical triumph.

scenes.setting: The corridor outside the private dining room at Gadsby's, near the main entrance—the gas lamps lower now, the noise of the public rooms diminished, the heat still oppressive. Clay has stepped out for air. The dinner is breaking up; men are collecting hats and calling for carriages.
scenes.goal: Clay, in the aftermath of his performance, seeks a moment of private satisfaction—and encounters the consequence of his indiscretion sooner than expected.
scenes.opposition: The Madisonian correspondent Fletcher, who approaches Clay in the corridor under the pretence of paying his respects, and in doing so reveals—through the precision of his quotation—that he has heard and recorded the 'His Accidency' remark.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Clay attempts to retract or soften the remark, he appears weak; if he confirms it, he hands Tyler's allies a weapon. He is trapped between pride and prudence in real time.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: The remark, once confirmed to the Madisonian correspondent, cannot be retracted. It will appear in print attributed to Clay, transforming a dinner-table witticism into a public insult that Tyler will treat as a declaration of personal war.
scenes.power_shift_target: Fletcher—a minor figure—gains enormous leverage simply by possessing the quotation. Clay, who dominated the dining room, is now at the mercy of a young journalist in a corridor.
scenes.turn: Clay, still buoyed by wine and the evening's triumph, does not deny the remark when Fletcher quotes it back to him. Instead, he smiles—and in that smile, Fletcher has his confirmation. Clay watches the young man walk away and feels, for the first time, the cold draught of a mistake he cannot undo.
scenes.consequences: The remark will appear in the Madisonian within days. Clay's moment of private reckoning—brief, suppressed, but real—establishes that he understands the cost even as he refuses to regret it. The chapter ends with Clay alone in the corridor, the dinner over, the damage done, and the knowledge that he has given Tyler the one thing a politically isolated president most needs: a grievance that will stiffen his spine.
scenes.emotional_beat: The hollow aftermath of triumph—the moment when exhilaration drains and the shape of the error becomes visible, like a reef at low tide.
scenes.continuity_notes: Fletcher is young, slight, carries a small notebook in his coat pocket. Clay is still holding his wine glass—he brought it from the dining room. Crittenden has already departed. Mangum left without saying goodnight to Clay. The time is approximately half past nine. Rives's letter is still in Clay's coat pocket, unmentioned since Scene 1. The corridor is poorly lit; only two gas lamps are burning.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Fletcher wants confirmation of the 'His Accidency' remark—not a denial, not a softening, but the smile or nod that transforms hearsay into quotable attribution.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Clay wants to assess how much Fletcher heard and whether the remark can be contained—but his pride and his wine conspire against his political instinct to deny.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Fletcher hides his awareness that the remark is politically explosive and that he is about to hand Tyler's allies their most potent rhetorical weapon. He presents himself as a young admirer paying respects.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Clay hides the first stirring of regret—the recognition that the remark, once in print, will transform his relationship with Tyler from political rivalry into personal enmity. He also hides from himself the degree to which wine has compromised his judgement.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Clay begins the encounter as the great senator condescending to a minor journalist; by the end, Fletcher possesses the only thing that matters—the confirmed quotation—and Clay is left holding an empty wine glass in a dim corridor.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: The encounter is about the relationship between power and language—the moment when a man's words escape his control and become the property of the world. Clay has spent his career wielding rhetoric as a weapon; now a single phrase, carelessly deployed, will be wielded against him.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Fletcher the Madisonian correspondent exerts pressure not through political power but through the simple act of possessing information—he represents the uncontrollable force of the press, which Clay has used as a weapon his entire career and which now turns in his hand.
scenes.opponent_actor: Fletcher, the Madisonian correspondent
scenes.opponent_move: Approaches Clay in the corridor and quotes the 'His Accidency' remark back to him with precise wording, framing it as admiration while seeking confirmation for publication.
scenes.pov_countermove: Smiles and does not deny the remark—a non-verbal confirmation that is both an act of pride and a political miscalculation, delivered with the deliberate ease of a man who will not be seen to retreat.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Fletcher publishes the remark attributed to 'a senior Whig senator' rather than Clay specifically—still damaging, but without the personal attribution that will make it a direct insult from Clay to Tyler. Clay's inaction (a denial or deflection) would have contained the damage.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: Fletcher is leaving Gadsby's now; whatever Clay says or does not say in the next sixty seconds determines how the remark will appear in print.
scenes.required_end_hook: The Madisonian will publish the remark within days, and Tyler—already suspicious of Clay's intentions—will receive it as a personal humiliation that transforms constitutional disagreement into implacable enmity. Clay stands alone in the corridor knowing the word has escaped him, and that the veto, when it comes, will carry the weight of wounded pride as well as constitutional conviction.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Clay lifts the wine glass to his lips, finds it empty, and holds it there a moment longer than sense requires before lowering it slowly—the gesture of a man who has run dry and knows it.
scenes.physical_interruption: A carriage wheel strikes a rut in the avenue outside, and the sharp crack through the open door startles Clay mid-thought, his hand tightening involuntarily on the glass stem.

scenes.setting: The President's study, second floor of the White House, late afternoon in late July 1841. The room is stifling, curtains half-drawn against the sun, the enrolled bill lying on the desk amid scattered correspondence.
scenes.goal: Tyler seeks to read the enrolled Fiscal Bank Bill methodically and find constitutional grounds to justify signing it, hoping to avoid the catastrophe of a veto.
scenes.opposition: Thomas Ewing arrives with a written memorandum from the cabinet majority urging Tyler to sign, and presses for a commitment before the weekend.
scenes.immediate_risk: Ewing may extract a verbal promise to sign that Tyler cannot later retract without appearing faithless.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: If Tyler gives Ewing any affirmative signal, word will reach Clay within hours and be published as a presidential commitment, foreclosing the veto option.
scenes.power_shift_target: Tyler — he must end the scene having preserved his freedom of action without openly defying his cabinet.
scenes.turn: Ewing places the cabinet memorandum on the desk and states that all six secretaries concur that the bill is constitutional. Tyler picks up the memorandum, reads it silently, then sets it aside without comment and asks Ewing whether the cabinet also concurs that the President's oath binds him to his own constitutional judgment or to theirs.
scenes.consequences: Ewing departs without the commitment he sought. Tyler is now aware that the cabinet is acting in concert — a coordinated pressure campaign, not individual counsel. This hardens his suspicion that Clay is directing the cabinet.
scenes.emotional_beat: Controlled dread beneath a surface of judicial calm.
scenes.continuity_notes: The enrolled bill is physically on Tyler's desk. Ewing's memorandum is a new document — must be referenced in later scenes. Time is late afternoon; Tyler has not yet read the bill closely this day. Ten-day signing window is running.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Ewing wants Tyler to commit verbally to signing the bill before the weekend so he can report success to Clay.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Tyler wants to understand the full scope of the cabinet's coordinated pressure without revealing that he is leaning toward a veto.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: That Clay personally drafted the cabinet memorandum's key constitutional argument and that the resignations are already being discussed as a contingency.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: That he has already identified the branching-without-consent clause as constitutionally fatal and is searching for a way to refuse without destroying his administration.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Ewing enters with institutional weight — six cabinet officers united. Tyler reclaims leverage by reframing the question from policy consensus to constitutional oath, a ground on which no cabinet majority can overrule him.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: This is a test of whether Tyler is a puppet or a president. Both men know it. Neither says so.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Henry Clay's unseen hand directing the cabinet's coordinated memorandum; Webster's conspicuous absence from the signatories is noted by Tyler.
scenes.opponent_actor: Thomas Ewing
scenes.opponent_move: Places the signed cabinet memorandum on Tyler's desk and states that all six secretaries regard the bill as constitutional and urge immediate signature.
scenes.pov_countermove: Reads the memorandum in silence, sets it aside, and poses a question that reframes the issue from cabinet consensus to presidential oath.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Ewing interprets silence as assent and departs to inform Clay that the President will sign.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: Ten-day constitutional window for signing or vetoing the bill; Ewing presses for a commitment before the weekend.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Tyler aligns the edges of the enrolled bill with the edges of the desk, squaring them precisely before responding to Ewing.
scenes.physical_interruption: A bead of sweat runs into Tyler's eye and he must blot it with his handkerchief mid-sentence, breaking the rhythm of his careful reply.

scenes.setting: The same study, early evening. Ewing has departed. Tyler is alone with the enrolled bill, a lamp now lit, the windows open to admit a faint breeze that does nothing against the heat.
scenes.goal: Tyler attempts to find a constitutional reading of the branching clause that would permit him to sign the bill in good conscience.
scenes.opposition: The text of the bill itself — specifically Section 16, which authorizes the bank's directors to establish branches in any state without requiring that state's legislative consent.
scenes.immediate_risk: Tyler may convince himself through wishful reasoning that the clause is ambiguous enough to permit signing, thereby surrendering his constitutional convictions under pressure.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: Tyler's self-respect and the constitutional principle of state sovereignty that has defined his entire political career.
scenes.power_shift_target: The bill — the text gains power over Tyler as he reads, then Tyler reclaims authority when he recognizes his own self-deception.
scenes.turn: Tyler catches himself constructing a tortured legal argument that the clause might be read as permissive rather than mandatory, realizes he is doing precisely what he accused the Jacksonians of doing with the Bank of the United States in 1834, and sets down his pen in disgust.
scenes.consequences: Tyler abandons the attempt to justify signing. The intellectual path to capitulation is now closed. What remains is the emotional and political reckoning with the veto's consequences.
scenes.emotional_beat: Self-disgust sharpening into moral clarity.
scenes.continuity_notes: Tyler is writing notes in the margin of his own copy of the bill. He references his 1834 Senate speech against Jackson's bank policy. The lamp is whale-oil. The pen is a quill, not steel-nib. Time is early evening; he has not eaten since midday.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Clay's constitutional argument, embedded in Ewing's memorandum, is the intellectual opponent Tyler is wrestling with — Clay's logic is seductive and nearly persuasive.
scenes.opponent_actor: Tyler's own temptation to rationalize signing — embodied in the memory of his 1834 Senate speech condemning exactly this kind of constitutional self-deception.
scenes.opponent_move: The branching clause's plain language resists every attempt at reinterpretation; Tyler's own marginal notes begin to echo the Jacksonian arguments he once denounced.
scenes.pov_countermove: Sets down the pen, pushes back from the desk, and speaks aloud to the empty room: a single sentence acknowledging that he cannot sign.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Tyler completes the tortured legal justification, commits it to paper, and finds himself unable to retract it once written.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The ten-day window continues to run.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Presses his thumb against the nib of the quill until the point bends, then carefully straightens it — a gesture of controlled frustration.
scenes.physical_interruption: His stomach growls audibly in the silent room, reminding him he has not eaten since midday, and for a moment the animal fact of hunger displaces all constitutional reasoning.

scenes.setting: The White House family quarters, late evening. Tyler has left the study and gone to the small parlor where Letitia Tyler rests in her invalid's chair. The room smells of camphor and lavender water.
scenes.goal: Tyler seeks the comfort of his wife's presence and, obliquely, her counsel — not on the law, but on whether he has the fortitude to endure what the veto will bring.
scenes.opposition: Letitia's illness — she can barely speak, and Tyler must read her responses in her eyes and the pressure of her hand. The opposition is also Tyler's own reluctance to burden a dying woman with political terror.
scenes.immediate_risk: Tyler may lose his resolve if Letitia's evident suffering reminds him that the veto will bring public attacks on his family.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: If Tyler allows fear for his family to override his constitutional judgment, he will sign the bill and lose himself.
scenes.power_shift_target: Tyler — he must draw strength from Letitia's silent presence.
scenes.turn: Letitia, who has not spoken a full sentence in weeks, squeezes Tyler's hand with unexpected force and says two words: 'Your duty.' Tyler understands that she knows, has always known, and that her judgment is the same as his own.
scenes.consequences: Tyler's private resolve is now anchored not only in constitutional principle but in the bedrock of his marriage. He returns to the study to begin drafting the veto message.
scenes.emotional_beat: Tenderness laced with grief — the intimacy of two people who know their time together is short.
scenes.continuity_notes: Letitia Tyler suffered a stroke in 1839 and is largely incapacitated. She will die in September 1842. She is present in the White House but rarely seen publicly. Camphor and lavender water are period-accurate sickroom scents. Tyler has not yet begun drafting the veto message.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: The absent but felt presence of their children, particularly Robert Tyler, who has been urging his father to find a compromise with Clay.
scenes.opponent_actor: Tyler's fear that the veto will expose his family to public hatred and political retaliation.
scenes.opponent_move: The sight of Letitia's wasted condition forces Tyler to confront the human cost of his decision — she will bear the abuse of the press without the strength to defend herself.
scenes.pov_countermove: Takes Letitia's hand and speaks to her plainly about the bill and what he intends to do, treating her as the political partner she has always been rather than shielding her as an invalid.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Tyler leaves the room without speaking of the bill, and his unshared burden festers into indecision through the night.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Tyler adjusts the blanket over Letitia's knees with excessive care, tucking the edges as though precision in this small act could compensate for his inability to protect her from what is coming.
scenes.physical_interruption: A floorboard creaks loudly under his weight as he crosses to her chair, and he freezes, absurdly afraid of having disturbed her, though she is awake and watching him.

scenes.setting: The President's study, past midnight. A single lamp burns. Tyler sits at the desk with fresh paper, the enrolled bill open beside him, Ewing's memorandum weighted down by a paperweight.
scenes.goal: Tyler begins drafting the veto message, committing his constitutional reasoning to paper in language that will withstand both legal scrutiny and political attack.
scenes.opposition: The knowledge that every sentence he writes is a nail in the coffin of his relationship with the Whig Party; the ghost of Clay's anticipated fury; the awareness that the message must be unanswerable on constitutional grounds or it will be dismissed as personal pique.
scenes.immediate_risk: Tyler may produce a message that is emotionally satisfying but constitutionally weak, giving Clay grounds to characterize the veto as arbitrary rather than principled.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: A poorly drafted veto message will be overridden by Congress and Tyler will have sacrificed his party standing for nothing.
scenes.power_shift_target: Tyler — by the end of the scene he must hold the moral and constitutional high ground through the strength of his own prose.
scenes.turn: Tyler writes the key sentence: that the bill's branching provision would compel sovereign states to accept federal instrumentalities they have not consented to receive, and that no president bound by his oath to the Constitution can affix his name to such a measure. The sentence comes not from legal reasoning but from the memory of his father's voice reading the Virginia Resolutions aloud. The personal and the constitutional fuse into a single conviction.
scenes.consequences: The veto message is begun. Tyler has crossed the point of no return. The document exists on paper. It can be discovered, leaked, or read. The act of writing has made the decision irrevocable.
scenes.emotional_beat: Grim resolve hardening into something approaching exhilaration — the terrible freedom of a man who has chosen his ground and will not be moved.
scenes.continuity_notes: Tyler's father, Judge John Tyler Sr., was a friend of Jefferson and a governor of Virginia. The Virginia Resolutions of 1798 are a touchstone of Tyler's constitutional philosophy. The veto message draft is now a physical object in the study. Time is past midnight; Tyler will not sleep this night. The ten-day clock continues.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Webster's qualified support for the bill haunts Tyler — Webster's constitutional opinion is the strongest intellectual counterargument Tyler must overcome in his own mind.
scenes.opponent_actor: The enrolled bill itself as an instrument of congressional will, and the anticipated constitutional counterarguments of Webster and Clay.
scenes.opponent_move: The bill's text resists Tyler's framing at every turn — each clause he addresses in the veto message reveals another layer of congressional intent designed to foreclose executive objection.
scenes.pov_countermove: Drafts a passage that turns the bill's own language against its authors, quoting the branching clause verbatim and demonstrating that it contradicts the Whig platform's stated respect for state sovereignty.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: The veto message remains unwritten and Tyler's resolve dissipates by morning, leaving him vulnerable to a renewed cabinet assault.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The ten-day signing window; Tyler is aware that each hour spent not signing is itself a political signal that will reach Clay by morning.
scenes.required_end_hook: Tyler finishes the first page of the veto message and hears, through the open window, the sound of a rider arriving at the White House gate after midnight — a messenger whose errand he does not yet know, carrying news that will complicate everything.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Tyler dips the quill, holds it suspended over the blank page for a long moment, then writes the first word with a deliberateness that is almost violent.
scenes.physical_interruption: A hand cramp seizes his writing hand after the first sustained paragraph, forcing him to set down the quill and flex his fingers, staring at the half-formed sentence as though it might complete itself.

scenes.setting: Webster's private residence on Louisiana Avenue, Washington, D.C. — his study, early morning, curtains half-drawn against the August heat, desk littered with diplomatic correspondence from Edward Everett in London and drafts of boundary proposals for the Maine-New Brunswick dispute.
scenes.goal: Webster is composing a careful letter to Edward Everett in London, establishing the framework for what will become the boundary negotiations, while simultaneously trying to gauge from the morning newspapers and a note from Ewing whether the cabinet is already organizing against Tyler before the veto is even announced.
scenes.opposition: Thomas Ewing's note, delivered by messenger, pressing Webster to attend an informal cabinet gathering that evening to discuss 'the present posture of affairs' — a transparent attempt to lock Webster into collective action before the veto drops.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Webster commits to attending Ewing's gathering, he will be drawn into a coordinated cabinet response before he knows Tyler's final decision, forfeiting his freedom of maneuver.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: Webster's diplomatic channel to Britain — the one achievement that justifies his remaining in office — becomes collateral damage in a cabinet revolt he joined prematurely.
scenes.power_shift_target: Webster — he must end the scene having preserved his independence from both factions.
scenes.turn: Webster's servant brings a second note — this one from the White House, requesting Webster's attendance upon the President at four o'clock that afternoon. The juxtaposition of the two summons forces Webster to choose which meeting to prioritize, and the White House note's formal phrasing suggests Tyler intends to share something of consequence.
scenes.consequences: Webster sends a carefully worded declination to Ewing's gathering, citing diplomatic correspondence that requires his immediate attention — a plausible excuse that is also a lie of omission, since the real reason is the White House summons. He has now separated himself from the cabinet's collective motion without openly breaking with them.
scenes.emotional_beat: Calculating dread — the sensation of a man who sees the storm coming and is not sure his shelter will hold.
scenes.continuity_notes: Webster has not yet been told of the veto. He suspects it from Tyler's recent coldness toward Ewing and from Clay's increasingly aggressive public statements. The Everett correspondence is real and ongoing — it must be referenced consistently as the diplomatic lifeline Webster is protecting. Time: early morning, approximately 7 a.m. The White House meeting is at 4 p.m. Ewing's gathering is set for that evening.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Ewing's note functions as institutional pressure from the Clay-aligned cabinet faction, attempting to conscript Webster into collective action before he can assess the situation independently.
scenes.opponent_actor: Thomas Ewing, via his written note demanding Webster's attendance at the cabinet gathering
scenes.opponent_move: Sends a note to Webster insisting on his presence at an informal cabinet meeting that evening to coordinate a unified response to Tyler's anticipated veto, framing absence as disloyalty to Whig principles.
scenes.pov_countermove: Drafts and dispatches a declination to Ewing citing urgent diplomatic correspondence with Everett as the reason for his unavailability, deliberately omitting any mention of the White House summons.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Ewing interprets silence as assent and lists Webster among those committed to attend, binding him publicly to the cabinet faction before Tyler reveals the veto.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The White House summons is for four o'clock that afternoon; Ewing's gathering is that evening. Webster must respond to Ewing before midday or his silence becomes consent.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Repeatedly dips his pen in the inkwell without writing, leaving small black pools on the blotter.
scenes.physical_interruption: The August heat forces him to loosen his cravat and mop his neck with a handkerchief, smearing ink from his fingers across the linen.

scenes.setting: The State Department offices, a cramped suite of rooms in a building adjacent to the Treasury, midday heat pressing through inadequate windows. Webster's desk is dominated by maps of the Maine-New Brunswick boundary and a stack of dispatches.
scenes.goal: Webster meets with his chief clerk, Fletcher Webster (his son), to review the state of the British boundary negotiations and ensure that the diplomatic work is sufficiently advanced to serve as both a genuine policy achievement and a political shield — proof that his presence in the cabinet serves national interests beyond partisan loyalty.
scenes.opposition: Fletcher Webster, who is alarmed by rumors circulating among State Department clerks that the entire cabinet will resign, presses his father to clarify his own intentions and warns that remaining in office after a mass resignation will make him a pariah among Whigs.
scenes.immediate_risk: Fletcher's agitation threatens to spread panic among the State Department staff; if clerks begin seeking other positions or leaking to the press, Webster's diplomatic preparations are compromised.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: If Fletcher cannot be steadied, the State Department's internal discipline collapses and Webster's negotiating position with Britain is weakened before it has properly begun.
scenes.power_shift_target: Webster — he must reassert authority over his own son and his own department.
scenes.turn: Fletcher produces a clipping from the National Intelligencer in which an anonymous Whig senator (clearly Mangum) suggests that any cabinet officer who remains after a veto is complicit in executive tyranny. Webster reads it, sets it down, and tells Fletcher that the boundary negotiation is more important than any senator's opinion — the first time he has articulated, even to his son, that he intends to stay.
scenes.consequences: Webster has now committed, at least within the privacy of his family, to remaining in the cabinet regardless of what the other secretaries do. Fletcher is not persuaded but is silenced. The diplomatic work continues. Webster's isolation from the Whig establishment deepens.
scenes.emotional_beat: Paternal authority masking private uncertainty — the father commanding confidence he does not entirely feel.
scenes.continuity_notes: Fletcher Webster serves as chief clerk of the State Department — this is historically accurate. The National Intelligencer clipping should reference Mangum obliquely but recognizably. Webster has not yet met with Tyler; the 4 p.m. meeting is still ahead. The boundary maps and Everett dispatches must be consistent with Scene 1.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Fletcher wants his father to commit to resigning with the rest of the cabinet, protecting the family's Whig standing and Fletcher's own career prospects.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Webster wants Fletcher to stop questioning his judgment and focus on the diplomatic work that justifies their continued presence in the administration.
