dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Fletcher hides the depth of his fear that his father's stubbornness will destroy them both socially and politically — and that he has already been approached by Ewing's people about the resignation plan.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Webster hides that he has not yet decided with certainty to stay — his declaration to Fletcher is as much an act of self-persuasion as it is a command.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Fletcher begins with moral leverage — the voice of family prudence and political realism. Webster reclaims authority by pivoting from defensive justification to offensive assertion: the treaty matters more than party loyalty. By the end, Fletcher is subordinate again, but unconvinced.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: This is a scene about a father trying to convince himself of his own courage by performing it for his son.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Mangum's anonymous newspaper attack creates public pressure that reaches Webster through his own son, making the political threat personal and familial.
scenes.opponent_actor: Fletcher Webster
scenes.opponent_move: Produces the National Intelligencer clipping and reads aloud the passage equating cabinet loyalty to Tyler with complicity in executive tyranny, then directly asks his father whether he intends to resign with the others.
scenes.pov_countermove: Takes the clipping from Fletcher's hand, sets it face-down on the desk, and states flatly that the Maine boundary negotiation is of greater consequence to the republic than any anonymous senator's opinion, ordering Fletcher to return to the dispatches.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Fletcher interprets his father's silence as indecision, leaves the office visibly shaken, and the State Department clerks observe his distress — rumors of Webster's imminent resignation begin circulating within the hour.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The 4 p.m. White House meeting is approaching; Webster must settle his department before leaving for it.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Aligns the boundary maps on his desk with excessive, deliberate precision, squaring corners that do not need squaring.
scenes.physical_interruption: A fly buzzes persistently near the ink-pot and Webster swats at it twice, missing both times, the small futility breaking the gravity of the moment.

scenes.setting: The White House, the President's private study on the second floor — a modest room with a writing desk, two chairs, and windows overlooking the south grounds. Late afternoon light slanting through half-closed shutters. Tyler is seated; Webster is shown in by a servant.
scenes.goal: Webster seeks to learn Tyler's final decision on the bank bill without appearing to have come to lobby for either outcome — he wants information, not commitment, so that he can calibrate his own position with maximum flexibility.
scenes.opposition: Tyler, who has already decided to veto the bill, intends to read Webster the draft veto message and gauge whether Webster will stand with him or join the coming revolt — this is a loyalty test disguised as a consultation.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Webster hedges or equivocates when Tyler reads the veto message, Tyler will conclude that Webster cannot be trusted and may demand his resignation along with the others — destroying Webster's diplomatic position.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: Webster loses his place in the cabinet, the British negotiations collapse, and his political career enters a terminal decline with no diplomatic triumph to redeem it.
scenes.power_shift_target: Tyler — he begins the scene as a president bracing for betrayal and ends it having secured the loyalty (or at least the silence) of the most prominent member of his cabinet.
scenes.turn: Tyler reads aloud from the draft veto message — not the political arguments, but the constitutional passage arguing that the President's oath requires him to exercise independent judgment on the constitutionality of legislation, regardless of party expectation. Webster, listening, recognizes that the argument is not merely defensible but is, in its essentials, correct — and that his own months of hedging have been a species of moral cowardice. He does not say this. He says: 'The argument is sound, Mr. President. I see no reason to counsel otherwise.'
scenes.consequences: Webster is now the only cabinet member who knows the veto is coming. He does not warn Clay. He does not warn Ewing. He does not warn Fletcher. His silence is a commitment more binding than any oath — he has chosen Tyler's constitutional position over Whig solidarity, though he would describe it as choosing the republic over faction. Tyler, for the first time, regards Webster not as a potential defector but as an ally. The isolation that will define Webster's next two years begins here.
scenes.emotional_beat: Recognition and surrender — the moment when a man who has been calculating discovers that the calculation has led him to a place that requires something closer to conviction.
scenes.continuity_notes: This is the chapter's central turn. Tyler has the draft veto message in hand — it must be a physical document he reads from, not a paraphrase. Webster has not eaten since morning (referenced in physical interruption). The room's shuttered light should be consistent with late afternoon in August. After this scene, Webster knows the veto is coming; no other cabinet member does. This knowledge asymmetry is the engine of the next chapter.

dialogue_dynamics.character_a_wants: Tyler wants Webster to hear the constitutional argument and declare his loyalty — or at least his acquiescence — before the veto becomes public. He needs one cabinet member of national stature to remain.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_wants: Webster wants to learn the decision without committing himself, preserving maximum flexibility. He enters the room intending to listen and defer judgment.
dialogue_dynamics.character_a_hides: Tyler hides the depth of his anxiety about governing alone — he presents the veto as serene constitutional conviction, but he is terrified of the political consequences and desperate for Webster's endorsement.
dialogue_dynamics.character_b_hides: Webster hides that he has already begun separating himself from the cabinet (the declined meeting with Ewing) and that his primary motive for staying is the British negotiation, not constitutional principle.
dialogue_dynamics.power_shift: Tyler begins in the weaker position — a president reading his own defense to a man who might reject it. But as the constitutional argument unfolds, its intellectual force shifts the balance. Webster's response ('The argument is sound') concedes the moral high ground to Tyler. By scene's end, Tyler holds the leverage: Webster has committed, and Tyler knows it before Webster fully does.
dialogue_dynamics.subtext: Two proud men negotiating the terms of an alliance neither would have chosen, discovering in the process that the constitutional question they are debating is also a question about their own integrity.

scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Clay's unseen but omnipresent expectations weigh on both men — Tyler knows Clay will treat the veto as a declaration of war, and Webster knows that his silence about the veto will be treated as treason by Clay's faction.
scenes.opponent_actor: John Tyler
scenes.opponent_move: Reads aloud the constitutional passage of the draft veto message and then sits in silence, watching Webster's face, waiting for a response that will determine whether he demands Webster's resignation or asks him to stay.
scenes.pov_countermove: After a measured pause, Webster states that the constitutional argument is sound and that he sees no reason to counsel otherwise — a commitment delivered in the language of legal assessment rather than political loyalty, preserving his dignity while conceding the point.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: Tyler interprets Webster's silence as disapproval, concludes he cannot be relied upon, and informs him that he will accept his resignation along with the rest of the cabinet — ending Webster's diplomatic career.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: The veto message will be sent to Congress within days; Tyler needs to know tonight whether Webster stands with him.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Webster sits perfectly still in his chair, hands resting on his knees, not reaching for the document Tyler offers him to read — letting Tyler's voice carry the argument rather than touching the paper himself.
scenes.physical_interruption: Webster's stomach clenches audibly in the silence after Tyler finishes reading — he has not eaten since early morning, and the sound intrudes grotesquely on the gravity of the moment.

scenes.setting: The walk from the White House back to Webster's residence on Louisiana Avenue — the streets of Washington in the early evening heat, the Capitol dome visible in the distance, the city's peculiar mixture of grandeur and squalor on display: unpaved streets, wandering livestock, the half-finished Washington Monument.
scenes.goal: Webster walks home in the August twilight, carrying the weight of what he has just committed to, and must decide whether to send word to Ewing or Clay about the coming veto — a warning that would preserve his Whig standing but betray Tyler's confidence.
scenes.opposition: His own divided loyalties — the pull of Whig solidarity and self-preservation against the constitutional argument he has just acknowledged as sound, and the diplomatic prize he cannot afford to lose.
scenes.immediate_risk: If Webster sends a warning to Clay or Ewing tonight, he betrays Tyler's confidence on the same day it was given — and Tyler will learn of it, because Washington leaks everything.
scenes.irreversible_cost_if_fail: If he warns Clay, he loses Tyler's trust permanently and gains nothing — Clay will not forgive him for staying in the cabinet regardless. If he stays silent, he loses his last chance to rejoin the Whig mainstream before the veto makes reconciliation impossible.
scenes.power_shift_target: Webster himself — this is the scene where his internal division resolves, however provisionally, into action (or deliberate inaction).
scenes.turn: Webster passes a lamplighter on Pennsylvania Avenue and thinks of writing to Clay — then remembers Tyler's voice reading the constitutional passage, the quiet certainty of it, and recognizes that warning Clay would not be loyalty but merely fear. He walks past the turning that would take him to Clay's lodgings and continues home. The decision is made by his feet before his mind has fully consented.
scenes.consequences: Webster's silence is now irrevocable — the evening passes, no warning is sent, and when the veto is announced, Webster will be the only cabinet member who knew and said nothing. His isolation from the Whig party is sealed. But so is his position with Tyler, and so is the British negotiation.
scenes.emotional_beat: Lonely resolve — the particular solitude of a man who has made a choice he cannot explain to anyone and is not certain he can explain to himself.
scenes.continuity_notes: It is evening, following the 4 p.m. meeting. Webster is walking — he did not take a carriage. Clay's lodgings are on a route Webster could plausibly pass. The lamplighter is a period-accurate detail for Washington streets. Webster has still not eaten. He arrives home to an empty study — Fletcher is not present (he was dismissed earlier). The Ewing gathering is happening this evening without Webster.
scenes.supporting_cast_pressure: Clay's physical proximity — his lodgings are on Webster's route home — creates a gravitational pull that Webster must resist through the simple act of not turning a corner.
scenes.opponent_actor: Self — the conviction that Whig loyalty demands warning Clay, set against the constitutional argument Tyler has just made and the diplomatic ambition Webster cannot relinquish.
scenes.opponent_move: The memory of Clay's voice at a dozen party councils, demanding unity, insisting that the Whig cause is greater than any individual scruple — and the knowledge that Ewing's gathering is happening right now, without him, and that his absence is already being noted.
scenes.pov_countermove: Walks past the turning to Clay's lodgings without stopping, continues home, enters his study, and sits at his desk without lighting a lamp or reaching for paper — choosing silence through the physical act of not writing.
scenes.failure_event_if_no_action: If Webster turns toward Clay's lodgings, he arrives to find Ewing and others already assembled, and is drawn into the collective resignation plan before the veto is even public — losing both Tyler's trust and his diplomatic position in a single evening.
scenes.deadline_or_clock: Ewing's cabinet gathering is happening tonight — every hour Webster delays his warning makes the eventual revelation of his foreknowledge more damning to his Whig allies.
scenes.required_end_hook: The veto message will reach Congress within days, and Webster is the only man outside Tyler's immediate household who knows it is coming — a secret that will define his reputation and his alliances for the remainder of the administration, and whose revelation will arrive in the next chapter like a cannon shot.
scenes.externalization_gesture: Sits at his desk in the dark study without lighting a lamp, hands flat on the wood surface, not reaching for pen or paper.
scenes.physical_interruption: A blister on his heel from the walk — his dress shoes were not made for the distance — forces him to pause and shift his weight at the precise moment he passes the turning toward Clay's lodgings.
